Additional features in a usable state that complement those delivered in previous iterations
A decomposition of all Product Backlog items into tasks or future Sprint Backlog lists

explanation: An Increment is a concrete stepping stone toward the Product Goal that must be usable and meet the Definition of Done, building additively on all prior Increments. It represents working, valuable product functionality—not designs, diagrams, test suites, or task breakdowns—that complements what was delivered before and could potentially be released.

question:
When do Developers take ownership of a Sprint Backlog item?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: During the Daily Scrum.
Whenever a team member can accommodate more work.
Never. All Sprint Backlog items are “owned” by the entire Scrum Team.
At the Sprint Planning Event.

explanation: The Sprint Backlog belongs to the Developers as a whole, and Scrum emphasizes the team as a self-managing, accountable unit rather than a collection of individuals with personally assigned work. Items are never owned by a single person; the Developers collaboratively decide who works on what and remain collectively accountable for delivering the Sprint Goal. This reflects the Scrum value of commitment and the principle that the whole team succeeds or fails together.

question:
Which of the following are feedback loops in Scrum?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective
Daily Standup, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective
Release Planning, Daily Scrum, and Sprint Review
Grooming, Daily Status, and Sprint Review

explanation: The Scrum events are designed as formal opportunities to inspect and adapt, which makes each of them a feedback loop. The Daily Scrum inspects progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapts the plan for the next day, the Sprint Review inspects the Increment and adapts the Product Backlog, and the Sprint Retrospective inspects how the work was done and adapts the team's processes. Note that "Daily Standup," "Grooming," "Daily Status," and "Release Planning" are not official Scrum terms or events.

question:
Who determines how to do the work during the Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Scrum Master.
The Technical Architects.
The Development Team.
The Team Leads.
The engineering director.

explanation: The Scrum Guide establishes that the Developers are self-managing, meaning they alone decide how to turn Product Backlog items into a working Increment. No one outside the Development Team can dictate the technical approach or working methods, which preserves their autonomy and accountability for delivering quality work each Sprint.

question:
During a Sprint, when is new work or further decomposition of work added to the Sprint Backlog?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: As soon as possible after they are identified
When the Product Owner identifies new work
During the Daily Scrum after the Developers approves it
When the Scrum Master has time to enter it

explanation: The Sprint Backlog is a living artifact that the Developers update throughout the Sprint as more is learned. Because it must reflect the most current understanding of the work needed to achieve the Sprint Goal, any newly identified work or further decomposition is added as soon as it emerges. This keeps the Sprint Backlog transparent and ensures it remains a real-time picture of the plan, rather than waiting for a specific event or person.

question: A product Increment must be released to production at the end of each Sprint.

options.text: True
False

explanation: A usable Increment must be produced each Sprint, but the Scrum Guide does not require it to be released to production. The decision of when to release is a business decision left to the Product Owner, who manages value delivery. The key requirement is that the Increment meets the Definition of Done and is in a potentially releasable state, regardless of whether it is actually deployed.

question:
What is the main reason for the Scrum Master to be at the Daily Scrum? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: They do not have to be there; they only need to ensure the Developers have a Daily Scrum.
To make sure every team member answers the three questions.
To write down any changes to the Sprint Backlog, including adding new items, and tracking progress on the burn-down.
To gather status and progress information to report to management.

explanation: The Daily Scrum is an event by and for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt their plan for the day. The Scrum Master's responsibility is to ensure the event takes place and stays within the timebox, but they are not required to attend or participate unless they are also acting as a Developer. The Daily Scrum is not a status meeting for management, and the Developers themselves are accountable for managing the Sprint Backlog and choosing how to conduct the event.

question:
A Scrum Team must have a Product Owner and Scrum Master.
(choose the best answer)

options.text: False. a Scrum Master is only necessary when requested or needed.
True. Each must be a full-time member on the Scrum Team.
True. Their participation and availability will impact the outcomes produced by the Scrum Team.
False. If a Product Owner is unavailable, he/she can be replaced by a Business analyst.

explanation: The Scrum Guide defines a Scrum Team as consisting of one Scrum Master, one Product Owner, and Developers—these are mandatory accountabilities with no sub-teams or hierarchies. The Product Owner's availability is essential for maximizing the value of the product and managing the Product Backlog, while the Scrum Master ensures Scrum is understood and enacted, fostering an effective and self-managing team. The presence and active engagement of both directly affect the team's ability to deliver valuable Increments each Sprint.

question:
What enhances best the transparency of an Increment?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Updating Sprint tasks properly in the electronic tracking tool.
Doing all work needed to meet the Definition of Done.
Reporting Sprint progress to the stakeholders daily.
Keeping track of and estimating all undone work to be completed in a "hardening" Sprint.

explanation: Transparency of an Increment is created by adhering to the Definition of Done, which establishes a shared, formal understanding of what it means for work to be complete. When all work meets the Definition of Done, the Increment is truly usable and its state is transparent to everyone, with no hidden or "undone" work disguising the real progress. Deferring incomplete work to a "hardening" Sprint actually reduces transparency by concealing the true condition of the product.

question:
Which statement is correct about the length of the Sprints?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: All Sprints must be one month or less and it is optimal to have a consistent Sprint length.
The Sprint length is dependent on the development effort forecasted (amount of items selected) during Sprint Planning.
The Sprint length is calculated by aggregating the time required to design, code and test.
The Sprint Length is defined by the Scrum Master.

explanation: Sprints are fixed-length events of one month or less that create consistency, and shorter Sprints generate more learning cycles and limit risk to a smaller time frame. A consistent Sprint length is preferred because it establishes a predictable cadence and makes forecasting, planning, and inspection easier. The Sprint length is not driven by the amount of work selected, nor calculated from task estimates, and it is a team-level decision rather than something dictated by the Scrum Master.

question:
How is management external to the Scrum Team involved in the Daily Scrum? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Scrum Master speaks on their behalf
Management gives an update at the start of each Daily Scrum
The Developers self-manage and are the only management required at the Daily Scrum
The Product Owner represents their opinions

explanation: The Daily Scrum is an event held by and for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt their plan for the day. As a self-managing team, the Developers internally decide who does what, when, and how, so no external management involvement is needed. Inviting outside managers to direct or participate would undermine the team's self-management, a core principle of Scrum.

question:
The timebox for the Sprint Planning event is? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Monthly.
4 hours.
Whenever it is done.
8 hours for a monthly Sprint. For shorter Sprints it is usually shorter.

explanation: Sprint Planning is timeboxed to a maximum of 8 hours for a one-month Sprint, and for shorter Sprints the event is usually shorter. This scaling principle applies to all Scrum events, ensuring the planning effort is proportional to the length of the Sprint being planned. The timebox sets a maximum duration, so the event can end sooner once its purpose is achieved.

question:
A Scrum Team selects a set of Product Backlog items for a Sprint Backlog with the intent to get these selected items Done by the end of the Sprint. Which three phrases best describe the purpose of a Definition of Done? 
(Choose the best three answers)

options.text: It defines what it takes for an Increment to be ready for release
It creates transparency over the work inspected in the Sprint Review
It tracks the percentage complete of a Product Backlog item
It guides the Developers in creating a forecast in the Sprint Planning
It provides a template for elements that need to be included in the technical documentation
It controls whether the developers have performed their tasks

explanation: The Definition of Done is a formal description of the state of the Increment when it meets the quality measures required for the product, meaning the work is complete and the Increment is potentially releasable. Because an Increment is only usable when it conforms to the Definition of Done, it creates transparency over what is actually inspected during the Sprint Review, ensuring stakeholders understand the true state of the work. It also guides the Developers during Sprint Planning, since they must know what "Done" means to accurately forecast how many Product Backlog items they can complete. The Definition of Done is about the quality of the Increment, not about tracking percentages, controlling individual tasks, or templating documentation.

question:
What are three benefits of self-management?
(Choose the best three answers)

options.text: Increased self-accountability
Increased accuracy of estimates
Increased commitment
Increased creativity
Increased rule compliance

explanation: Self-management means the Scrum Team internally decides who does the work, how, and when, rather than being directed by someone outside the team. This ownership naturally fosters greater accountability, as the team owns its decisions and outcomes, and stronger commitment, since people are more invested in goals they help shape. It also unlocks creativity, because cross-functional teams empowered to determine their own approach can innovate freely rather than simply follow imposed rules or focus on estimate precision.

question:
What is the key concern when multiple Scrum Teams are working from the same Product Backlog?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Making sure there is enough work for everyone on each team
Maximizing velocity
Clear definition of requirements
Minimizing dependencies between teams
Meeting original scope projections

explanation: When multiple Scrum Teams work from the same Product Backlog, they must coordinate to integrate their work into a single, valuable Increment each Sprint. Dependencies between teams create bottlenecks and integration risks that undermine each team's ability to deliver a "Done," potentially releasable Increment independently. Minimizing these dependencies enables teams to remain self-managing and to deliver value with minimal friction, which is why it is the key concern.

question: Multiple Scrum Teams working on the same product or system all select the same Product Backlog.

options.text: True
False

explanation: When multiple Scrum Teams work on the same product, they share a single Product Backlog to ensure a coherent, unified view of the work and a shared understanding of what delivers value. A product has only one Product Backlog and one Product Owner accountable for it, regardless of how many teams contribute, which keeps priorities aligned and prevents conflicting or duplicated work across teams.

question:
Why does the Product Owner want the Developer to adhere to the definition of "Done?" 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: To be able to reprimand the Team when they don't meet their velocity Goal for the Sprint.
To have complete transparency into what has been done at the end of each Sprint.
To know what the Team will deliver over the next three Sprints.
To predict the Team's Productivity over time.

explanation: The definition of "Done" creates a shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete, ensuring transparency over the Increment. When everyone adheres to the same definition, the Product Owner can trust that work reported as finished truly meets the agreed quality standard and is potentially releasable. This transparency supports empiricism, allowing inspection and adaptation based on a reliable view of what has actually been accomplished each Sprint.

question:
Every Scrum Team must have a Product Owner and a Scrum Master.
(choose the best answer)

options.text: False, a Scrum Master is only required when asked for by the Development Team.
True, outcomes are affected by their participation and availability.
True, Each must be 100% dedicated to their Scrum Team.
False, a Product Owner can be replaced by a business analyst in the Development Team.

explanation: The Scrum Guide defines the Scrum Team as consisting of three accountabilities: the Product Owner, the Scrum Master, and Developers—all of which are mandatory with no sub-teams or hierarchies. The Product Owner's accountability for maximizing product value and the Scrum Master's accountability for the team's effectiveness directly influence the outcomes the team delivers, so their presence and engagement matter. While both roles are essential, the Scrum Guide does not require either to be 100% dedicated to a single team, which is why dedication is not the deciding factor.

question:
What would be the main benefits of self-organization?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Increased rule compliance, self-accountability, commitment.
Increased rule compliance, self-accountability, output.
Increased capacity, accuracy of estimates, output.
Increased creativity, self-accountability, commitment.

explanation: Self-organizing teams choose how best to accomplish their work rather than being directed by others, which fosters creativity in problem-solving and decision-making. When a team owns its decisions, members take greater self-accountability for outcomes and develop stronger commitment to the goals they've collectively agreed upon. This aligns with the Scrum value of Commitment and the empirical principle that those doing the work are best positioned to decide how to do it.

question:
Who does the work to make sure Product Backlog items conform to the Definition of Done?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The Quality Assurance Team
The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Developers
The Scrum Team

explanation: The Developers are the members of the Scrum Team committed to creating any aspect of a usable Increment each Sprint. The Scrum Guide explicitly assigns them accountability for instilling quality by adhering to a Definition of Done. There is no separate quality assurance team in Scrum, and the Product Owner and Scrum Master have distinct accountabilities that do not include performing the development work itself.

question:
When does the second Sprint start?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Once the architectural changes for the second Sprint have been approved by the senior architect
Immediately after the first Sprint
After the Product Backlog for the second Sprint has been selected
After the customer completes acceptance testing of the first Sprint

explanation: Sprints are continuous and back-to-back, with a new Sprint starting immediately after the conclusion of the previous one. There are no gaps between Sprints for activities like architectural approvals, separate acceptance testing phases, or pre-selecting the backlog, as Sprint Planning to define the work occurs at the start of the new Sprint itself. This cadence ensures a constant rhythm and continuous delivery of value.

question:
What is the time-box for the Sprint Review?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: 2 hours for a monthly Sprint
4 hours for a monthly Sprint
1 day
4 hours and longer as needed
As long as need

explanation: The Sprint Review is time-boxed to a maximum of four hours for a one-month Sprint, with proportionally shorter durations for shorter Sprints. As a Scrum event, it has a fixed maximum length to keep it focused and prevent waste, ensuring the inspection of the Increment and adaptation of the Product Backlog happens efficiently.

question:
The Developers ask their Product Owner to re-order the Product Backlog. The Scrum Team is waiting for an external supplier to deliver a specific software component. Without that component there won’t be enough work in the next Sprint to occupy the full team. The Product Owner asks the Scrum Master for help. What would be good advice to give the Product Owner?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Tell the Product Owner to re-order the Product Backlog so the work involving the external component can be planned in a separate Sprint
Tell the Product Owner that the Product Backlog should be ordered to maximize utilization of the Developers
Remind the Product Owner that their primary concern is the flow of value reflected in the ordering of the Product Backlog

explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product, and this is expressed through ordering the Product Backlog to optimize the flow of value—not to keep Developers fully utilized. Ordering by value rather than resource utilization ensures the team works on what is most valuable next. Maximizing utilization is not a Scrum concern and can lead to building low-value items simply to fill capacity.

question: Cross-functional teams are optimized to work on one component or layer of a system only.

options.text: True
False

explanation: Cross-functional teams possess all the skills necessary to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team. Rather than being limited to a single component or layer, they are organized to deliver a complete, usable Increment of product. Teams optimized around a single component or layer are component teams, which create dependencies and hinder the delivery of a "Done," potentially releasable Increment.

question:
What would be the responsibilities of a self-organizing Development Team?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Update stakeholders on the daily progress and keep the burn-down chart updated.
Select the Product Backlog items for the Sprint and do the work planned in the Sprint Backlog.
Writing User Stories and reordering the Product Backlog.
Deciding on the value of the product features and value estimations.

explanation: A self-organizing Development Team chooses how to accomplish their work rather than being directed by others, which includes selecting how many Product Backlog items to pull into the Sprint and crafting the plan (Sprint Backlog) to deliver the Sprint Goal. Determining the value of features and ordering the Product Backlog are accountabilities of the Product Owner, not the Development Team. By owning what they commit to and how the work gets done, the team embodies the self-management principle central to Scrum.

question:
What are two effective ways for a Scrum Team to ensure security concerns are satisfied?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Delegate the work for the security department
Postpone the work until a specialist can perform a security audit and create a list of security-related Product Backlog items
Add a Sprint to specifically resolve the security concerns
Add security concerns to the Definition of Done
Have the Scrum Team create Product Backlog items for each concern

explanation: Adding security concerns to the Definition of Done ensures that every Increment meets the required quality and security standards before it is considered complete, embedding security as a non-negotiable part of "Done." Creating Product Backlog items for each concern makes the security work transparent and allows it to be ordered, refined, and addressed by the Developers within normal Sprints. Both approaches keep accountability within the cross-functional Scrum Team rather than delegating it externally or deferring it to a separate phase, which would violate the principle that the team has all the skills needed and that every Sprint produces a usable, high-quality Increment.

question:
What is the time-box for the Sprint Planning? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Whenever it is done
Monthly
8 hours for a monthly Sprint
4 hours for a monthly Sprint

explanation: Sprint Planning is time-boxed to a maximum of eight hours for a one-month Sprint. For shorter Sprints, the event is usually shorter, as the time-box scales proportionally to the Sprint's length. This time-box ensures the team has enough time to collaboratively plan the work while preventing the event from becoming inefficient or open-ended.

question:
Which of these may a Scrum Team deliver at the end of a Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Failing unit tests, to identify acceptance tests for the next Sprint.
A valuable, useful Increment that meets Definition of Done.
An Increment of software with minor known bugs in it.
A single document, if that is what management asked for.

explanation: Each Sprint must produce at least one usable, valuable Increment that conforms to the Definition of Done, which establishes the quality measures required for the work to be considered complete. Work that does not meet the Definition of Done—such as software with known defects or failing tests—cannot be released or presented as a done Increment. The purpose of every Sprint is to deliver something potentially releasable that provides value, not merely a document to satisfy a request.

question:
The Scrum Teams should have all the skills needed to:
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Complete the project within the date and cost as calculated by the Product Owner
Turn Product Backlog items into an Increment of potentially releasable product functionality (valuable and useful Increment).
Do all of the development work, except for specialized testing that requires additional tools and environments

explanation: Scrum Teams are cross-functional, meaning they collectively possess all the skills necessary to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team. This enables them to turn Product Backlog items into a "Done," potentially releasable Increment, which is the core purpose of every Sprint. Relying on external specialists for any part of the work, or framing the goal around fixed dates and costs, contradicts the self-managing and cross-functional nature of the team.

question:
When multiple Scrum Teams are working on the same product, 
should all of their Increments be integrated every Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Yes, but only for Scrum Teams whose work has dependencies.
Yes, in order to accurately inspect what is done.
No, that is far too hard and must be done in a hardening Sprint.
No, each Scrum Team stands alone

explanation: When multiple Scrum Teams work on the same product, they share a single Product Backlog, Product Goal, and Definition of Done, and their combined work must result in a valuable, usable Increment by the end of each Sprint. Integrating all Increments every Sprint ensures the work is genuinely "Done" and allows stakeholders to inspect the true state of the product at the Sprint Review, enabling empirical decisions and adaptation. Deferring integration to a "hardening Sprint" hides undone work and undermines transparency, a core pillar of empiricism.

question:
The Product Owner determines how many Product Backlog items the Developers select for a Sprint. 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: False
True, but only after confirmation by the resource manager that the team has enough capacity
False, the Scrum Master does that
True, accordingly to what was committed to the stakeholders
False, capacity and commitment are the project manager’s responsibility
True

explanation: The Developers alone are responsible for determining how much work they select for a Sprint, as they are the ones who will perform the work and best understand their own capacity. The Product Owner may influence priorities and help the team understand and select items that best achieve the Sprint Goal, but cannot dictate how many Product Backlog items are pulled into the Sprint. This reflects the self-managing nature of the Scrum Team, where the Developers own the decisions about how much work they can accomplish.

question:
Which of the following services is appropriate for a Scrum Master in regard to the Daily Scrum? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Teach the Developers to keep the Daily Scrum within the 15 minute timebox.
All answers apply.
Facilitate in a way that ensures each team member has a chance to speak.
Ensure that all 3 questions have been answered.
Lead the discussions of the Developers.

explanation: The Daily Scrum is an event by and for the Developers, so the Scrum Master does not lead, facilitate the discussion, or dictate its structure. The Scrum Master's role is to ensure the event takes place and to coach the Developers in keeping it within its 15-minute timebox. The "three questions" are no longer prescribed by the Scrum Guide, and the Developers are responsible for conducting the event however they choose, as long as it focuses on progress toward the Sprint Goal.

question: Scrum is a methodology that tells in detail how to build software incrementally?

options.text: True
False

explanation: Scrum is intentionally a lightweight framework, not a detailed methodology or prescriptive set of techniques. It defines only the roles, events, and artifacts needed for empirical process control, while deliberately leaving the specific practices for building software up to the teams that use it. This allows various processes, techniques, and methods to be employed within the framework.

question:
If burndown charts are used to visualize progress, what do they track?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Accumulated business value delivered to the customer
Work remaining across time
Individual worker productivity
Accumulated cost

explanation: A burndown chart is a forecasting tool that plots the amount of work remaining against time, helping the Scrum Team visualize progress toward completing the work in the Sprint or product. The Scrum Guide describes such projective practices as ways to assess trends and predict whether the remaining work can be completed within the desired timeframe, not as measures of cost, business value, or individual performance.

question:
Which three behaviors demonstrate that a Scrum Team is self-managing?
(Choose the best three answers)

options.text: The Scrum Team members are working within the boundaries of their functional description and nicely handing off work from analyst to developer to tester to integration
The Scrum Team has all the skills needed to create an Increment
Developers collaboratively selecting their own work during the Sprint.
The Scrum Master is no longer needed.
Stakeholder are attending the Daily Scrum to check progress and work with the Scrum Master to optimize the functional scope of the Sprint.
The Developers creating their own Sprint Backlog, reflecting all work that is part of the Definition of Done

explanation: A self-managing Scrum Team internally decides who does what, when, and how, rather than being directed by others outside the team. This requires the team to be cross-functional with all the skills needed to create an Increment, so they don't depend on external specialists or hand-offs. It also means the Developers themselves choose how to turn Product Backlog items into Increments by creating and owning their Sprint Backlog and collaboratively selecting their own work, which reflects the accountability and self-organization the Scrum Guide places on the Developers.

question:
When does a Sprint conclude? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: When the Product Owner decides enough has been delivered to meet the Sprint Goal.
When the Sprint Retrospective is complete.
When all the tasks are completed by the Developers.
When all Product Backlog items meet their Definition of Done.

explanation: The Sprint is a container event that holds all other Scrum events, and the Sprint Retrospective is the final event within it, concluding the Sprint. A Sprint does not end early because work is done or the Sprint Goal is met, nor does it extend if work remains incomplete—Sprints have a fixed time-box. Once one Sprint ends with the Retrospective, the next Sprint begins immediately.

question:
A Scrum Team is required to deliver a done Increment by the end of a Sprint. 
Select two statements that explain what Done means.
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Whatever the Product Owner defines as quality.
No work left from the Definition of Done.
Ready for integration.
All work the Developers are willing to do.
All work to create useful and valuable Increment.

explanation: "Done" means the Increment meets the Definition of Done—the formal description of the state of the Increment when it satisfies the quality measures required for the product—so no remaining work is left to complete that standard. The purpose of each Sprint is to produce a usable, valuable Increment, meaning all work necessary to make it useful and ready for release has been completed. Quality is not arbitrarily set by the Product Owner alone, nor limited to what Developers are willing to do; it is governed by the agreed Definition of Done.

question: The purpose of a Sprint is to produce a done Increment of working product

options.text: True
False

explanation: Each Sprint's purpose is to deliver a "Done," usable, and potentially releasable Increment that meets the Definition of Done. This aligns with Scrum's empirical foundation, where each Sprint produces a valuable piece of working product to inspect and adapt against the Product Goal. The Increment must be in a usable condition regardless of whether the Product Owner decides to release it.

question:
What is the Product Owner responsible for during the Sprint Retrospective?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Capturing requirements for the Product Backlog
Participating as a Scrum Team member
Summarizing and reporting the discussions to the stakeholders that they represents in the Scrum Team
The Product Owner should not take part in Sprint Retrospectives

explanation: The Sprint Retrospective is an event for the entire Scrum Team, which includes the Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Developers. As a full member of the Scrum Team, the Product Owner participates in inspecting how the last Sprint went regarding individuals, interactions, processes, tools, and the Definition of Done, and helps plan improvements. The Retrospective is not a venue for gathering requirements or reporting to stakeholders, and excluding the Product Owner would contradict the collaborative, whole-team nature of the event.

question:
What is the role of Management in Scrum? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Continually monitor staffing levels of the Scrum Team.
Identify and remove people that are not working hard enough.
Monitor the progress of the Developers on the Scrum Team.

Support the Product Owner with insights and information into high value product and system capabilities. Support the Scrum Master to cause organizational change that fosters empiricism, self-management, bottom-up intelligence, and intelligent product delivery.

explanation: Management's role in Scrum is supportive rather than directive—they help the Product Owner with insights into valuable product and system capabilities and back the Scrum Master in driving organizational change toward empiricism and self-management. The Scrum Team is self-managing, meaning they internally decide who does the work and how, so management does not monitor individual progress, staffing, or "hard work." Such command-and-control behaviors would undermine the Scrum Values of courage and respect and the self-management that empiricism depends on.

question:
What factor should be considered when establishing the Sprint length?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The need for the team to learn based on doing work and measuring results.
The organisation’s release schedule.
The frequency at which team formation can be changed.
The organization has mandated similar length Sprints.

explanation: Sprint length should be short enough to limit risk and enable empirical process control, which relies on transparency, inspection, and adaptation. Choosing a Sprint length based on the team's ability to learn from doing work and inspecting the results supports this empiricism, allowing the team to adapt frequently and keep risk within acceptable bounds. External factors like release schedules or organizational mandates are not the primary driver; the Sprint is a feedback loop that fosters continuous learning and improvement.

question:
The Developers has suggested to move the Daily Scrum to every other day instead of every day.
What is the most appropriate action for the Scrum Master to take?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Learn why the Developers wants this, coach the team on why the Daily Scrum is important and work with them to improve the outcome of the Daily Scrum.
Support the self-organizing team’s decision.
Have the Development Team members vote on which days the Daily Scrum should occur.

explanation: The Daily Scrum is a prescribed event held every day to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the plan, so changing it to every other day violates the Scrum framework's rules. While the Developers are self-managing in deciding *how* to do their work, they cannot eliminate or alter the cadence of mandatory Scrum events. The Scrum Master serves the team by coaching them on the purpose and value of the Daily Scrum, addressing the underlying concern that makes it feel unnecessary, and helping them make the event more effective.

question:
Why should a Scrum Team adhere to its Definition of Done
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: To have complete transparency into what has been done at the end of each Sprint
To know what the team will deliver over the next three Sprints
To be able to reprimand the team when they don’t meet their velocity goal for the Sprint
To predict the teams productivity over time

explanation: The Definition of Done creates a shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete, ensuring transparency over the Increment produced each Sprint. This commitment guarantees that everyone inspecting the Increment understands exactly what has been accomplished and whether it meets quality standards. Transparency is a foundational pillar of Scrum's empirical process, enabling effective inspection and adaptation.

question:
When is the Sprint Backlog created?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: During Sprint Planning
At the beginning of the project
During refinement
During the Sprint Retrospective

explanation: The Sprint Backlog is created during Sprint Planning, where the Scrum Team forms the Sprint Goal, selects Product Backlog items for the Sprint, and creates a plan for delivering them. It is composed of the Sprint Goal (the why), the selected Product Backlog items (the what), and the actionable plan to deliver the Increment (the how). While it emerges and is updated throughout the Sprint as more is learned, its initial creation happens during Sprint Planning, the event that initiates each Sprint.

question: A Scrum Master fulfills the same role a traditional Project Manager

options.text: True
False

explanation: The Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the Scrum Team and the organization, not a traditional Project Manager who directs work, assigns tasks, and controls scope, schedule, and budget. The Scrum Master coaches the team in self-management and cross-functionality, helps everyone understand Scrum Theory and practice, and removes impediments—but does not command-and-control the team or own the work. Accountabilities like prioritizing work belong to the Product Owner, while the Developers decide how to do the work, distributing responsibilities that a Project Manager would traditionally hold.

question:
Which of the following is an example of an Increment?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: A plan for the overall product release.
A mock-up of the product marketing materials.
A design for the product.
A product roll-out plan.
A valuable, useful set of products featured
All of the above.

explanation: An Increment is a concrete stepping stone toward the Product Goal that is usable and adds value to the previously created Increments. Each Increment must be in usable condition and meet the Definition of Done, meaning it represents a working, valuable piece of the product rather than a plan, design, mock-up, or roll-out document. Plans and designs are inputs or supporting artifacts, not the actual product value delivered to stakeholders.

question:
Which statement best describes the Sprint Backlog as outcome of the Sprint Planning? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: It is a complete list of all work to be done in a Sprint
It is ordered by the Product Owner
It is the Developers plan for the Sprint
Each task is estimated in hours
Every item has a designated owner

explanation: The Sprint Backlog is the Developers' plan, created by and for the Developers to deliver the Sprint Goal—it is a highly visible, real-time picture of the work they plan to accomplish during the Sprint. Unlike the Product Backlog, which the Product Owner orders, the Sprint Backlog belongs entirely to the Developers. It emerges and adapts throughout the Sprint as more is learned, so it is never a fixed or complete list, and the Scrum Guide does not require estimating tasks in hours or assigning individual owners to items.

question:
When does the next Sprint begin?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: When the Product Owner is ready.
Next Monday.
Immediately following the next Sprint Planning.
Immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint.

explanation: Sprints are continuous and back-to-back, with a new Sprint starting immediately after the conclusion of the previous one. There are no gaps between Sprints, ensuring a consistent and sustainable cadence of work. This reflects the time-boxed, iterative nature of Scrum, where the rhythm of delivery is never interrupted.

question:
Why are the events held at the same time and same place? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The consistency reduces complexity.
The place can be named.
The Product Owner demands it.
Rooms are hard to book and this lets it be booked in advance.

explanation: The Scrum Guide states that events should be held at the same time and place to reduce complexity. By establishing this consistency, the team eliminates the need to renegotiate logistics each time, removing unnecessary decisions and creating a predictable cadence that supports the empirical process.

question:
Which techniques could the Scrum Master use when the Scrum Team gets caught in an internal disagreement about which agile practices to apply?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Involve the complete Scrum Team in making a decision.
Use coaching techniques such as open questions and active listening
Ask an external agile coach what they recommend.
Ask team members to take the issue up with the company’s Human Resources department.

explanation: The Scrum Master serves the team as a coach in self-management and cross-functionality, so using open questions and active listening helps the team explore options and reach their own conclusions rather than imposing solutions. Because Scrum Teams are self-managing, decisions about how to work—including which practices to adopt—should be made by the whole team, fostering commitment and ownership. Deferring to an external coach or escalating to HR undermines the team's self-management and bypasses the collaborative problem-solving that Scrum Values like courage, respect, and openness encourage.

question:
How often should Scrum Team membership change?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: As needed, while taking into account a short-term reduction in productivity.
As needed, with no special allowance for changes in productivity.
Every Sprint to promote shared learning.
Never, it reduces productivity.

explanation: Scrum Teams should remain stable to build trust, cohesion, and a sustainable pace, but they are not frozen—membership can change when there is a genuine need. However, the Scrum Guide notes that changing team composition generally reduces productivity in the short term as the team re-establishes its working relationships and self-management. Recognizing this trade-off allows the organization to make informed decisions about when changes are truly warranted.

question:
What is the order of items in the Product Backlog? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The less clear items at the top I
The recently added items at the top
Alphabetical
Less valuable and most unclear items at the bottom

explanation: The Product Backlog is an ordered list, with the Product Owner responsible for ordering it to best achieve goals and maximize value. Higher-priority, more valuable items are placed at the top where they are clearer, more detailed, and more refined, while lower-value and less understood items naturally settle toward the bottom. This ordering supports just-in-time refinement, ensuring the most important work is ready to be selected first during Sprint Planning.

question:
As the Developers start work during the Sprint, they realize they have selected too much work to finish the Sprint. What should they do?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Inform the Product Owner at the Sprint Review, but prior to the demonstration.
Find another Scrum Team to give the excess work to.
Reduce the Definition of Done and get all of the Product Backlog items done by the new definition
As soon as possible in the Sprint, work with the Product Owner to remove some work or Product Backlog items.

explanation: The Sprint Backlog is an emergent plan that the Developers adjust throughout the Sprint as they learn more. When scope needs to change, the Developers renegotiate it with the Product Owner, who owns the value and priority of the work. Acting as soon as possible reflects Scrum's empirical pillars of transparency and adaptation, while keeping the Definition of Done intact preserves quality and the integrity of the Increment.

question:
As the Sprint Planning meeting progresses, the Developers see that the workload is greater than they can handle. Which two are valid actions.
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: The Developers work overtime during this Sprint
The Developers ensure that the Product Owner is aware, start the Sprint, and monitor progress
Recruit additional Developers members before the work can begin
Remove or change selected Product Backlog items
Cancel the Sprint

explanation: During Sprint Planning, the Developers and Product Owner collaboratively negotiate the scope of work, so adjusting which Product Backlog items are selected for the Sprint is a valid response when the workload exceeds capacity. Equally valid is keeping the Product Owner informed of the realistic forecast, starting the Sprint, and adapting as more is learned, since the Sprint Backlog is a forecast that emerges throughout the Sprint. Working overtime or adding people mid-effort does not address the core issue and contradicts sustainable pace and stable team principles, and only the Product Owner can cancel a Sprint—and only when the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete.

question:
When Developers are having trouble delivering a working Increment because they don’t understand a functional requirement, what should they do?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Partially complete the functionality, and discuss the remaining work at the Sprint Review
Defer the work to a more appropriate Sprint
Collaborate with the Product Owner to determine what is possible and acceptable
Add a specialist to the Developers

explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product and is the authority on what the requirements mean and what is acceptable. Since Scrum is built on collaboration and the Scrum Values of openness and commitment, Developers should immediately work with the Product Owner to clarify the requirement rather than guessing, deferring, or delivering partial work. This ensures the Sprint Goal remains achievable and the Increment meets the Definition of Done and delivers real value.

question:
When does a Sprint end?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: When there is no work remaining in the Sprint Backlog.
When the time-box expires.
It depends on the Product Owner.
Immediately after the conclusion of the Sprint Review.

explanation: A Sprint is a fixed time-box of one month or less, and this duration is what defines when it ends. The Sprint concludes when its time-box expires regardless of whether all the planned work was completed, because Sprints have consistent durations to provide predictability and rhythm. Even if work remains in the Sprint Backlog or is finished early, the Sprint still runs until its set time-box is reached.

question:
What are two ways that regulatory compliance issues are dealt with in Scrum?
(Choose the two best answers)

options.text: They are discussed, determined, and documented before the actual feature development Sprints.
They are addressed along with functional development of the product
They are added to the Product Backlog and addressed in early Sprints, while always requiring at least some business functionality, no matter how small
They are addressed by a separate team who is responsible for compliance issues

explanation: Regulatory compliance is treated as work like any other requirement, so it belongs on the Product Backlog and is addressed alongside functional development rather than handled separately or fully up front. Every Sprint must produce a "Done," potentially releasable Increment that delivers value, which means even compliance-focused Sprints must include at least some business functionality. Splitting compliance off to a separate team or completing it entirely before development contradicts the cross-functional, integrated nature of the Scrum Team and the principle of delivering value incrementally each Sprint.

question:
Which two things are appropriate for a Scrum Master to do if the Developers do not have the engineering tools and infrastructure to completely finish each selected Product Backlog item? 
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Have the Developers establish a Definition of Done that is actually possible to achieve given current circumstances
Declare the Developers not ready for Scrum
Coach the Developers to improve their skills, tools and infrastructure over time and adjust the Definition of Done accordingly
Refocus the current Sprint on establishing the Developers infrastructure instead of delivering an Increment
Encourage the Product Owner to accept partially done Increments until the situation improves

explanation: The Definition of Done must reflect the team's current reality so that the Increment it produces is genuinely usable and transparent—creating a Definition that cannot be met would undermine transparency, a pillar of empiricism. As a servant-leader, the Scrum Master coaches the Developers to incrementally strengthen their skills, tools, and infrastructure, allowing the Definition of Done to be expanded over time toward higher quality. Lowering quality by accepting partial work, or halting value delivery entirely, would violate Scrum's commitment to producing a "Done," potentially releasable Increment each Sprint.

question:
Who creates the Definition of Done?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The Product Owner since they are responsible for the product's success.
If it is not an organizational standard, the Scrum Team must create a Definition of Done appropriate for the product.
The Scrum Team, in a collaborative effort where the result is the common denominator of all members' definitions.
The Scrum Master since they are responsible for the productivity of the Developers.

explanation: The Definition of Done creates a shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete and ensures transparency of the Increment. According to the Scrum Guide, if the Definition of Done is part of an organizational standard, all Scrum Teams must follow it as a minimum; if it is not an organizational standard, the Scrum Team must create one appropriate for the product. It is not the sole responsibility of any single accountability (such as the Product Owner or Scrum Master), nor is it a "common denominator" compromise of individual definitions.

question:
Where should the Daily Scrum be held according to Scrum?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Wherever the Development Team thinks is best.
In the coffee corner to confirm this is an informal gathering.
Around the Scrum board.
In the room that is reserved by the Scrum Master.
In a room where management can listen in.

explanation: The Scrum Guide does not prescribe a specific location for the Daily Scrum; it only specifies that the event is held at the same time and place each day to reduce complexity. As the Daily Scrum is by and for the Developers, they are the self-managing team members responsible for deciding how best to plan their day, including where to meet. This reflects the Scrum principle of self-management, empowering the team to organize their own work without external direction.

question:
How do you know that a Scrum Team is cross-functional?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Every member of the Scrum Team is able to perform every task.
There are no conflicts within the Scrum Team.
The Scrum Team has all the skills to create an Increment by the end of every Sprint
A few of the Developers pair program and do Test Driven Development.

explanation: A cross-functional team possesses all the competencies needed to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team. This is about the team collectively having the necessary skills—not every individual being able to do everything. Cross-functionality enables the Scrum Team to be self-managing and deliver a usable, Done Increment by the end of each Sprint.

question:
A Scrum Team has been working on a product for 9 Sprints. A new Product Owner comes in, understanding he is accountable for the Product Backlog. However, he is unsure about his responsibilities. Which two activities are part of the Product Owner role according to Scrum?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Providing the Developers with detailed specifications
Describing features as use cases
Interacting with stakeholders
Creating detailed functional test cases
Ensuring that the most valuable functionality is produced first, at all times.

explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product, which includes ordering the Product Backlog so the most valuable work is done first. The Product Owner also represents the needs of stakeholders and is a key point of engagement between the Scrum Team and stakeholders, making stakeholder interaction central to the role. Writing detailed specifications, use cases, or test cases is not a Product Owner responsibility—the Developers self-manage how the work gets done and decide on the technical details themselves.

question:
If burndown charts are used to visualize progress, what does a trend line through a release burndown chart indicate?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: When the project will be over if the Product Owner removes work that is equal in effort to any new work that is added.
The evolution of the cost spent on the project.
When all work will be completed so the Scrum Team can be released for the other work.
When the work remaining will likely be completed if nothing changes on the Product Backlog or the Developers

explanation: A release burndown chart plots the work remaining over time, and a trend line projects when that remaining work will likely reach zero based on the team's current pace. This projection is only valid under the assumption that conditions stay constant — meaning no changes to the scope of the Product Backlog or to the Developers doing the work. Because the Product Backlog is emergent and constantly evolving, the trend line is merely a forecast, not a guarantee, and it must be reassessed as items are added, removed, or re-estimated.

question:
When should a Sprint Goal be created?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: At any time during the Sprint
It should have been created in the previous Sprint during Product Backlog refinement
It must be established before Sprint Planning in order to begin planning
During Sprint Planning
A Sprint Goal is not mandatory in Scrum

explanation: The Sprint Goal is one of the outputs of Sprint Planning, where the Scrum Team collaboratively crafts it as part of defining why the Sprint is valuable (Topic One of the three Sprint Planning topics). It must be finalized before the planning event ends, providing coherence and focus that guides the Developers as they select Product Backlog items and shape the Sprint Backlog. The Sprint Goal is a mandatory commitment to the Sprint Backlog, not something created beforehand or arbitrarily during the Sprint.

question:
The CEO asks the Developers to add a "very important" item to a Sprint that is in progress. 
What should the Developers do? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Add the item to the next Sprint.
Inform the other members of the Scrum Team so the team can decide what to do.
Add the item to the current Sprint without any adjustments.
Add the item to the current Sprint and drop an item of equal size.

explanation: Only the Product Owner is accountable for managing the Product Backlog and the value of the work, so Developers cannot simply accept new work injected mid-Sprint by anyone, including the CEO. The Scrum Team self-manages and decides together how to handle changes, with the Product Owner and Developers negotiating scope while protecting the Sprint Goal. Raising this with the whole team respects the empirical process and the boundaries of accountabilities rather than the Developers unilaterally altering the Sprint.

question:
What enhances the transparency of an Increment? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Doing all work needed to meet the Definition of Done
Keeping track of and estimating all undone work to be completed in a separate Sprint
Reporting Sprint progress to the stakeholders daily
Updating Sprint tasks properly in the electronic tracking tool

explanation: The Definition of Done creates transparency by providing a shared, formal understanding of what work must be completed for an Increment to be considered usable and releasable. When all work meets the Definition of Done, everyone has a clear, consistent view of the Increment's true state, eliminating hidden or incomplete work. Partially done work or carrying undone work into a separate Sprint undermines this transparency, as it obscures the real progress and quality of the product.

question:
For which of the following is the Scrum Master responsible? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Properly adopting and using the Scrum framework
Managing performance of the Scrum Team
Keeping track of resource allocation
The meetings and the objectives that a Scrum Team sets for itself

explanation: The Scrum Master is accountable for establishing Scrum as defined in the Scrum Guide, helping everyone understand Scrum Theory and practice both within the team and the organization. This makes the Scrum Master a true leader who serves the Scrum Team and the larger organization in adopting and using the framework effectively. The other options misrepresent the role: the Scrum Master does not manage performance, allocate resources, or set the team's objectives, since the Scrum Team is self-managing and the Product Owner owns the Product Goal.

question:
Who starts the Daily Scrum?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Whoever the Developers decide should start
The person coming in last. This encourages people to be on time and helps to stay within the time-box
The person who has the token
The Scrum Master. This ensures that the Developers have the meeting and stay within the time-box
The person who last broke the build

explanation: The Daily Scrum is an event owned by the Developers, who are responsible for self-managing how they conduct it. The Scrum Guide deliberately avoids prescribing a specific format, technique, or facilitator so the Developers can decide what works best for them, including who starts it. This reflects the Scrum principle of self-management, where the team organizes its own work rather than having it dictated by the Scrum Master or arbitrary rules.

question:
Which two statements best describe a Sprint Goal?
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: Sprint Goals are defined when the completed work is reviewed at the end of a Sprint.
Sprint Goals give the Development Teams flexibility and creativity on how to implement functionality during the Sprint.
During the Sprint Planning, the entire Scrum Team collaboratively crafts a Sprint Goal based on a business objective that the Product Owner would like to achieve that Sprint.
Sprint Goals can change as new insights emerge during the Sprint.

explanation: The Sprint Goal is the single objective for the Sprint, crafted collaboratively by the entire Scrum Team during Sprint Planning based on the Product Owner's desired business objective; it becomes a commitment of the Sprint Backlog. Because the goal describes the "what" and "why" rather than prescribing exact tasks, it gives the Developers flexibility and creativity in deciding how to deliver the functionality. The Sprint Goal itself remains fixed for the duration of the Sprint to provide focus and coherence, even though the scope of work may be renegotiated as more is learned.

question:
Which role would know the most about the progress toward a business objective or a release, and be able to explain clearly the alternatives?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Product Owner
The Release Manager
The Developer
The Scrum Master

explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product and managing the Product Backlog, which makes them responsible for tracking progress toward larger objectives such as business goals or releases. Because they own the ordering of the backlog and understand the product vision and stakeholder needs, they are best positioned to explain progress and present alternatives. Roles like Release Manager do not exist in Scrum, and the Scrum Master and Developers do not hold accountability for product value or release-level decisions.

question:
Which of the following are appropriate topics for discussion during a Sprint Retrospective?
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: Documenting acceptance criteria for items in the next Sprint
How the team collaborates
Identifying high priority process improvements for the next Sprint
The order of items in the Product Backlog

explanation: The Sprint Retrospective exists to inspect how the last Sprint went with regards to individuals, interactions, processes, tools, and the Definition of Done, so examining how the team collaborates is central to its purpose. The Scrum Team also identifies the most helpful changes to improve its effectiveness, planning ways to increase quality and process improvements for the next Sprint. In contrast, documenting acceptance criteria and ordering the Product Backlog are Product Backlog refinement and Sprint Planning activities, not Retrospective topics.

question:
Developers are self-managing, which of the following do they manage?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Product Backlog ordering.
When to release, based on its progress.
Sprint Backlog.
Stakeholders for the Sprint Review.
Sprint length.

explanation: The Developers own and manage the Sprint Backlog, as they are the ones who plan the work needed to achieve the Sprint Goal and adjust their plan throughout the Sprint. Product Backlog ordering and release decisions are accountabilities of the Product Owner, while the Sprint length (timebox) is a fixed property defined within Scrum. Self-management means the Developers decide who does what, when, and how within the Sprint, which is reflected in their ownership of the Sprint Backlog.

question:
Which are characteristics of the Daily Scrum?
(choose 2 best answers)

options.text: Its purpose is to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog.
It is facilitated by the team lead
It is free from and designed to promote conversation
It consists of the Scrum Master asking the team for status
Its location and time remain constant
It is held first thing in the morning

explanation: The Daily Scrum is an event for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog as necessary, adjusting their plan for the upcoming day's work. To reduce complexity and create consistency, it is held at the same time and place every working day of the Sprint. It is not a status meeting reported to the Scrum Master or a team lead, and while it can be held in the morning, the Scrum Guide does not mandate any specific time of day other than that it remain constant.

question:
A Scrum Team is a cohesive unit of professionals that consists of which of the following?
(Choose all that apply)

options.text: Customers.
One Product Owner.
Developers.
One Scrum Master.

explanation: The Scrum Guide defines the Scrum Team as consisting of one Scrum Master, one Product Owner, and the Developers—there are no sub-teams or hierarchies within it. Customers are stakeholders outside the Scrum Team and are not members of it. This single, cohesive unit holds all the skills and accountabilities necessary to create value each Sprint.

question:
One of the Scrum events is the Daily Scrum. What are two intended outcomes of the Daily Scrum? 
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: An updated Scrum board to make Sprint progress transparent for the Stakeholders.
A status report for the upper Management indicating what each individual has done, will
An update of completed tasks and of the remaining work so the Scrum Master can plan the next day.
A shared understanding of the most important work to be undertaken next to achieve the best possible progress toward the Sprint Goal.
Identify new impediments for the Scrum Master to take care of.

explanation: The Daily Scrum is an event for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt their plan, producing a shared understanding of the most important work needed next to make the best possible progress. It is not a status meeting for management or the Scrum Master, nor is it about reporting individual tasks—it serves the Developers themselves. Surfacing impediments that may block progress is a natural and valuable outcome, which the Scrum Master then helps remove to keep the team focused on the Sprint Goal.

question:
Which three of the following are feedback loops in Scrum
(choose the best three answers)

options.text: Release Planning
Refinement Meeting
Sprint Retrospective
Sprint Review
Daily Scrum

explanation: The Scrum events are designed to create transparency and provide formal opportunities for inspection and adaptation, which are the essence of feedback loops. The Daily Scrum lets the Developers inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt their plan daily, the Sprint Review inspects the Increment with stakeholders to adapt the Product Backlog, and the Sprint Retrospective inspects how the team worked to adapt its processes and practices. Release Planning is not a Scrum event, and Refinement is an ongoing activity rather than a formal inspect-and-adapt feedback loop.

question:
A Scrum Master is introducing Scrum to a new Team. The Team has decided that a Sprint Retrospective is unnecessary. What action should the Scrum Master take?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Consult with the Product Owner to see how they feel about the situation.
Comply with the decision of the self-managing team.
Call a meeting between the Scrum Team and senior management.
Begin facilitating productive and useful Sprint Retrospectives

explanation: The Sprint Retrospective is a mandatory event in Scrum that exists to inspect the Scrum Team's effectiveness and plan improvements, embodying the empirical pillars of inspection and adaptation. Self-management does not permit a team to discard the foundational rules and events defined in the Scrum Guide. As a true leader who serves the team, the Scrum Master is responsible for ensuring all Scrum events take place and are positive, productive, and kept within their timebox, so they should help the team experience the value of an effective Retrospective.

question:
What are two ways that architecture and infrastructure are handled in Scrum?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: They are built by a separate team through the creation of an architectural runway
They are implemented along with functional development of the product
They are discussed, determined and documented before the actual feature development Sprints
They are added to the Product Backlog and addressed in early Sprints while always requiring at least some business functionality, no matter how small

explanation: In Scrum, every Sprint must produce a "Done," usable Increment that delivers value, so architecture and infrastructure cannot be built in isolation or as a separate up-front phase. Instead, they emerge incrementally and are implemented alongside functional development, ensuring that even early architectural work is tied to delivering at least some business functionality. This reflects Scrum's empirical approach and emergent design, where the architecture evolves through the work rather than being fully determined and documented before development begins. Handling these elements through the Product Backlog keeps a single prioritized list of work managed by the Product Owner and avoids the dependencies and waste of separate teams or phases.

question:
Who is part of the discussion about which Product Backlog items are selected for a Sprint?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The Developers
The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Scrum Team

explanation: Sprint Planning is a collaborative event in which the entire Scrum Team participates. The Product Owner ensures the team understands the most valuable work and helps clarify priorities, the Developers determine how much they can take on and craft the Sprint Goal alongside the rest of the team, and the Scrum Master facilitates the event. Selecting Product Backlog items for the Sprint is therefore a shared discussion among all members of the Scrum Team, not a decision made by any single role.

question:
Which statement best describes the Sprint Review? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: It is a demo at the end of the Sprint for everyone in the organization to check on the work done.
It is a mechanism to control Developer activity during a Sprint.
It is when the Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect the outcome of a Sprint and figure out what to do next.

explanation: The Sprint Review is a working session, not merely a demo, where the Scrum Team and key stakeholders inspect the Increment and the progress toward the Product Goal. Together they collaborate on what was accomplished and what has changed in the environment, and they adjust the Product Backlog to determine the next steps. This embodies the empirical pillars of inspection and adaptation while fostering transparency and collaboration with stakeholders.

question:
How should Product Backlog items be chosen when multiple Scrum Teams work from the same Product Backlog?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Developers pull in work in agreement with the Product Owner
Each Scrum Team takes an equal number of items
The Product Owner decides
The Product Owner should provide each team with its own Product Backlog
The Scrum Team with the highest velocity pulls Product Backlog items first

explanation: When multiple Scrum Teams work from the same Product Backlog, they share a single Product Owner who is accountable for ordering and maximizing the value of that one Product Backlog. The Developers are responsible for selecting how much work they pull into their Sprint, since they best understand their own capacity, but this selection must be done in collaboration with the Product Owner to respect the Product Backlog's order and the overall Product Goal. Splitting items equally, by velocity, or giving each team a separate backlog would undermine transparency and the single source of truth that one Product Backlog provides.

question:
Who is responsible for the sizing of Product Backlog items?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The Developers, alone.
The Scrum Master
The Developers after clarifying requirements with the Product Owner.
The Product Owner with input from the Developers
The most senior people in the organization, including architects and subject matter experts.

explanation: The Scrum Guide states that the Developers, who will perform the work, are responsible for sizing (estimating) Product Backlog items because they have the technical knowledge to do so. However, sizing requires understanding what the items entail, so the Developers collaborate with the Product Owner to clarify requirements and reach a shared understanding before estimating. This reflects the principle that those doing the work make the estimate, while the Product Owner provides clarity on the "what" and "why."

question:
Every Scrum Team should have:
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: At least 1 representative from each major software engineering discipline (like QA, DEV, UX)
The competencies and skills needed to deliver a done Increment in a Sprint
One lead developer and no more than 8 other members

explanation: Scrum Teams are cross-functional, meaning they collectively possess all the competencies and skills necessary to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team. The Scrum Guide does not prescribe specific roles like QA, UX, or a lead developer within the Developers, nor does it mandate representatives from particular disciplines. What matters is that the team as a whole can deliver a "Done" Increment every Sprint.

question:
What would be the best two ways to identify that a Development Team is self-organizing?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Management is able to know which members are working on which items.
Creativity thrives and new possibilities are explored.
The Development Team members are cross-functional and knows what it needs to do to deliver the all tasks committed to the Sprint Planning.
The Development Team is able to resolve internal conflicts between members in order to continue working.

explanation: A self-organizing Development Team chooses how best to accomplish its work rather than being directed by others outside the team, which naturally fosters an environment where creativity flourishes and new possibilities are explored. Self-organization also means the team takes ownership of its own dynamics, including resolving internal conflicts independently so it can keep delivering value. Management knowing who works on what would reflect external control, which contradicts self-organization, and the Scrum Team forecasts work for a Sprint rather than committing to deliver all tasks.

question:
What are two responsibilities of testers on a Scrum Team?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Verifying the work of programmers
Scrum has no “tester” role
Finding bugs
Everyone in the Scrum Team is responsible for quality
Tracking quality metrics

explanation: Scrum recognizes only three accountabilities—Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Developers—and does not define a separate "tester" sub-role or title within the team. Instead, the Developers are cross-functional and collectively accountable for creating a quality Increment each Sprint that meets the Definition of Done. Quality is a shared responsibility of the whole Scrum Team rather than something delegated to specialized individuals, reinforcing the value of collaboration and accountability.

question:
What tactics should a Scrum Master use to divide a group of 100 people into multiple Scrum Teams?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Ask the Product Owner to assign the people to teams.
Ask the people to divide themselves into teams.
Create teams based on their skills across multiple layers (such as database, UI, etc.)

explanation: Scrum is built on self-management, which means the people doing the work should organize themselves and decide how they form their teams. Allowing the developers to self-organize into teams fosters ownership, commitment, and respect—core Scrum Values—and typically produces more effective, cross-functional teams than having a single person dictate the structure. Dividing teams by technical layers would create component teams rather than the cross-functional teams Scrum requires to deliver a "Done" Increment each Sprint.

question:
Who has the final say on the order of the Product Backlog? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The Developers.
The Product Owner.
The Scrum Master.
The Stakeholders.
The CEO.

explanation: The Product Owner is solely accountable for managing the Product Backlog, which includes ordering its items to best achieve goals and maximize the value of the product. While the Product Owner may consult Developers, stakeholders, or others for input, they alone hold the final say on ordering—reinforcing the single, clear point of accountability the Scrum Guide establishes. For the Product Owner to succeed, the entire organization must respect their decisions.

question: Multiple Scrum Teams working on the same product or system all select work from the same Product Backlog

options.text: True
False

explanation: When multiple Scrum Teams work on the same product, they must share a single Product Backlog. The Scrum Guide states that a product has one Product Backlog and one Product Owner, which provides a single source of truth and ensures alignment toward one common Product Goal. Each team then selects work from this shared backlog during Sprint Planning.

question: A high performing Scrum Team will have frequent Release Sprints.

options.text: True
False

explanation: There is no such thing as a "Release Sprint" in Scrum. Every Sprint must produce a potentially releasable Increment that meets the Definition of Done, so a high-performing Scrum Team is capable of releasing at any time—even multiple times within a single Sprint—without dedicating special Sprints to releasing. Reserving separate Sprints for releasing would contradict the goal of delivering valuable, usable Increments each Sprint.

question:
The Daily Scrum is an event that happens every day. What would be three key concerns if the frequency were to be lowered to every two or three days?
(Choose the best three answers)

options.text: Impediments are raised and resolved more slowly
The Sprint Backlog may become inaccurate
Opportunities to inspect and adapt the Sprint Backlog are lost
The Scrum Master loses the ability to update the Gantt chart properly
Too much work is spent updating the Scrum board before the meeting
The Product Owner cannot accurately report progress to the stakeholders

explanation: The Daily Scrum is the Developers' opportunity to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog as necessary, adjusting the plan for the next day's work. Reducing its frequency means the Developers inspect and adapt less often, so the Sprint Backlog drifts out of date between sessions and no longer reflects the real plan. It also delays surfacing impediments, since the Daily Scrum is where blockers are commonly identified, so they linger longer and threaten the Sprint Goal. The other options reflect non-Scrum artifacts (Gantt charts) or misunderstand who is responsible for progress and the empirical, just-in-time nature of the event.

question:
Which output from Sprint Planning provides the Scrum Team with a target and overreaching direction for the Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Sprint Backlog
The Sprint Goal
Sprint Review minutes
The release plan

explanation: The Sprint Goal is the single objective for the Sprint, created during Sprint Planning, that provides the Scrum Team with a coherent target and overarching direction for the work. While the Sprint Backlog contains the specific items to be worked on, it is the Sprint Goal that gives meaning and focus to those items, enabling the team to collaborate flexibly toward a shared purpose rather than working on a set of unrelated tasks.

question:
The IT manager asks the Developers for a status report describing the progress throughout the Sprint. The Developers ask the Scrum Master for advice. The Scrum Master should/will:
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Create and deliver the report to the manager herself.
Tell the Developers to figure it out themselves
Tell the Developers to fit the report into the Sprint Backlog.
Talk to the IT manager and explain that the progress in Scrum comes from inspecting an Increment at the Sprint Review.
Ask the Product Owner to send the manager the report.

explanation: The Scrum Master serves the organization by coaching stakeholders to understand and embrace empirical Scrum practices, removing the impediment of expecting traditional status reports. In Scrum, progress is made transparent through a working Increment inspected at the Sprint Review, where stakeholders collaborate on what was done rather than reading written status updates. This protects the Developers from disruptions and helps the organization adopt an empirical approach based on inspecting actual outcomes.

question:
When must a Scrum Team release each Increment?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: After every Sprint, withouut exception.
When the Scrum Team finishes their work.
When it makes sense to release it.
Whenever the product is free of defects.

explanation: An Increment must meet the Definition of Done and be potentially releasable by the end of every Sprint, but this does not mean it must be released every Sprint. The Product Owner decides when releasing the Increment delivers the most value, so the actual release happens when it makes sense from a business and value perspective.
