Multiple Increments may be created within a single Sprint and released independently as appropriate.

question: Cross-functional teams are specialized for working on specific system components (e.g. design, database, backend, frontend)

options.text: True
False

explanation: Cross-functional teams are not specialized by component; rather, they possess all the skills necessary to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team. Specializing the team around specific system components creates dependencies and silos, which undermines the team's ability to deliver a complete, working Increment. The Scrum Guide emphasizes that Developers are cross-functional, collectively holding the diverse skills needed to turn Product Backlog items into a usable product.

question: To get started in terms of what to build, Scrum requires no more than a Product Owner with enough ideas for a first Sprint, Developers to implement those ideas and a Scrum Master to help guide the process

options.text: True
False

explanation: Scrum is intentionally lightweight and designed to start with minimal upfront work; it requires only a Product Owner with enough of a Product Backlog to fuel a first Sprint, Developers to turn those ideas into an Increment, and a Scrum Master to foster understanding and effective use of Scrum. The framework relies on empiricism, so the product evolves through inspection and adaptation across Sprints rather than requiring exhaustive planning before beginning. This reflects Scrum's emphasis on getting started and learning by doing.

question:
When is a Sprint over? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: When all Product Backlog items meet their Definition of Done.
When the timebox expires.
When the Product Owner says it is done.
When all the tasks are completed.

explanation: A Sprint is a fixed-length timebox, and it ends when that timebox expires—never sooner and never later. The duration is consistent throughout development, providing the regular cadence and predictability that empirical process control relies on. The Sprint is not extended to finish remaining work, nor cut short because work appears complete, which is why only the expiration of the timebox defines its end.

question:
In order to achieve the benefits of Scrum, it is important to enact the value of commitment. What 
two actions demonstrate the commitment of Scrum Team members? 
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: Do your best.
Work late
Send outa daily status report.
Always deliver the Items in the Sprint forecast.
Help the other Scrum Team members.

explanation: Commitment in Scrum means dedicating oneself to the goals of the Scrum Team and supporting one another, not promising to deliver a fixed scope or working excessive hours. Doing your best reflects personal dedication to quality and the team's objectives, while helping fellow team members embodies the collaborative, self-managing nature of the Scrum Team. The Sprint forecast (Sprint Backlog) is a plan that may change as more is learned, so guaranteeing delivery of every item contradicts Scrum's empirical, adaptive approach.

question:
The Product Owner must release each Increment to production. 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Without exception
To make sure the Developers make it done every Sprint
When it makes sense
Whenever the product is free of defects

explanation: Each Increment must meet the Definition of Done and be usable, but the decision to actually release it to production is based on value and timing—it happens when it makes sense for the product and stakeholders. The Scrum Guide states that an Increment may be delivered to stakeholders before the end of the Sprint, but the Sprint Review should never be considered a gate to releasing value. Therefore, releasing every Increment is not mandatory; the Product Owner exercises judgment about when delivery provides the most value.

question:
Who has the final decision on the order of items in the Product Backlog? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Scrum Team
The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
The Stakeholders.
The Developers.

explanation: The Product Owner is solely accountable for managing the Product Backlog, which includes ordering its items to best achieve goals and maximize the value of the product. While the Product Owner may delegate work or take input from stakeholders, the Developers, and others, the final decision on ordering remains with the Product Owner. For this accountability to succeed, the entire organization must respect their decisions.

question:
A Sprint Retrospective should be held:
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: At the beginning of each Sprint.
At the end of the last Sprint in a project or a release.
Only when the Scrum Team determines it needs one.
At the end of each Sprint.

explanation: The Sprint Retrospective is a mandatory event that occurs at the end of every Sprint, after the Sprint Review and before the next Sprint Planning. Its purpose is to allow the Scrum Team to inspect how the last Sprint went regarding individuals, interactions, processes, tools, and the Definition of Done, then create a plan for improvements. Because it is a regular, recurring event tied to each Sprint, it cannot be skipped or held only when the team feels it is needed, ensuring continuous inspection and adaptation.

question:
What is the role of management in Scrum?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: To facilitate the Scrum Teams with insights and resources that help them improve.
To identify and remove people that aren’t working hard enough.
To continually monitor staffing levels of the Developers.
To monitor the Scrum Team’s productivity.

explanation: Scrum Teams are self-managing, meaning they internally decide who does the work, when, and how, rather than being directed by managers. Management's role is to support the team by providing the resources, organizational environment, and insights that enable continuous improvement, not to monitor productivity or police individual effort. This reflects Scrum's foundation on empiricism and the value placed on empowered, self-organizing teams.

question:
Which Scrum Value is affected by a lack of trust in the Scrum Team?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Focus
Respect
Openness
Courage
Commitment
All of the above

explanation: Trust is the foundation upon which all five Scrum Values rest, so its absence undermines every one of them. Without trust, team members cannot be open about their work and challenges, hesitate to show courage in tackling tough problems, lose respect for one another's capabilities, struggle to commit to shared goals, and become distracted from their focus. The Scrum Guide emphasizes that when the values of commitment, focus, openness, respect, and courage are embodied and lived, trust is built—conversely, eroding trust damages them all.

question:
When does a Developer become the sole owner of an item or task on the Sprint Backlog?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Whenever a team member can accommodate more work.
During the Daily Scrum.
At the Sprint Planning event.
Never. All Sprint Backlog items are "owned" by the Developers on the Scrum Team.

explanation: The Sprint Backlog belongs to the entire group of Developers collectively, not to any single individual. Scrum emphasizes shared accountability and self-management, where the Developers as a whole are responsible for turning the Sprint Backlog into a valuable Increment. No work is assigned or owned individually, which supports collaboration and the team's collective commitment to the Sprint Goal.

question:
What is the accountability of the Product Owner during Sprint 0?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: There is no such thing as Sprint 0.
Create the overall release plan to ensure that organizational goals are met.
Determine the composition of the Scrum Team to meet the release plan.
Gathering, eliciting and analyzing the requirements to build the Product Backlog.
Make sure enough Product Backlog items are refined to fill the first 3 Sprints.

explanation: The Scrum Guide defines no concept of a "Sprint 0"; all Sprints are consistent, time-boxed iterations of equal length that each must deliver a usable, valuable Increment. The notion of a preparatory Sprint dedicated solely to upfront planning, requirements gathering, or release plotting contradicts Scrum's empirical, iterative nature, where work emerges and is refined throughout the development effort. Therefore, asking about a Product Owner's accountability during "Sprint 0" is based on a false premise.

question:
Which three questions might be answered by the Developers at the Daily Scrum?
(choose the best three answers)

options.text: What will I do today to help the Scrum Team meet the Sprint Goal?
What will I be working on tomorrow?
How many hours did I spend on the project yesterday?
How is the Sprint proceeding?
Why were you late?
Do I see any impediment that prevents me or the Developers from meeting the Sprint Goal?
What did I do yesterday that helped the Scrum Team meet the Sprint Goal?

explanation: The Daily Scrum is held by the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog as necessary, so questions focused on what work will be done today to meet the Sprint Goal and what impediments might block achieving it directly support this purpose. While the Scrum Guide no longer mandates the "three questions" format, these examples keep the conversation centered on the Sprint Goal and creating an actionable plan for the next day's work. Tracking individual hours, status reporting, or addressing tardiness are not the focus of the event, which is about the team's collective progress rather than individual accountability.

question:
When is a Product Backlog item considered complete?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: When the item meets the Definition of Done
When the item passes all acceptance criteria
At the end of the Sprint
When the item has gained product sponsor approval

explanation: A Product Backlog item is only considered complete when it satisfies the Definition of Done, which is the formal, shared understanding of the quality standards the Increment must meet to be usable and potentially releasable. While acceptance criteria define the conditions for a specific item, the Definition of Done establishes the broader transparency and quality commitment for all work, ensuring everyone agrees on what "Done" truly means. Items that do not meet the Definition of Done cannot be released or even presented at the Sprint Review as part of the Increment.

question:
During the Sprint, the Development Team realizes they might not be able to finish all of the items in the Sprint Backlog. What should happen?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Product Backlog items are either ‘done’ or ‘not done.’ This helps with transparency, reduces complexity and allows for improved empiricism.
Continue Sprinting until the work is complete and redefine a new Sprint time-box based on the results of the current Sprint.
The Sprint length holds and the Development Team continuously learns what is actually possible to do within the time-box.

explanation: The Sprint is a fixed time-box that does not get extended or shortened, which preserves consistency and the rhythm that enables empirical inspection and adaptation. When the Developers realize not all work will be completed, the scope may be renegotiated with the Product Owner, but the Sprint length remains intact. Over time, this discipline helps the Development Team improve their forecasting by learning what is realistically achievable within the time-box.

question:
During a Sprint Retrospective, for what is the Scrum Master responsible?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Prioritizing the resulting action items
Participating as a Scrum Team member and facilitating as requested or needed
Summarizing and reporting the discussions to management
Acting as a scribe to capture the Developer’s answers

explanation: The Scrum Master is a true member of the Scrum Team and participates in the Sprint Retrospective as a peer, while also being accountable for ensuring the event takes place and is productive. As a servant-leader, the Scrum Master facilitates the event when requested or needed, rather than directing it, prioritizing action items, or reporting discussions to management. The Retrospective is owned by the entire Scrum Team, so ownership of outcomes and decisions belongs to the team collectively, not the Scrum Master alone.

question:
Which are properties of the Daily Scrum?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: It is fifteen minutes or less in duration.
It is facilitated by the team lead.
It is free form and designed to promote conversation.
It consists of the Scrum Master asking the Team members the 3 questions.
Its location and time remain constant.
It is held first thing in the morning.

explanation: The Daily Scrum is timeboxed to 15 minutes to keep it focused and efficient, regardless of the Sprint's length. It is held at the same time and place every day to reduce complexity and remove the need for repeated planning around when and where to meet. The Daily Scrum is owned and conducted by the Developers, not facilitated by a "team lead," and the Scrum Guide no longer prescribes the three questions—the Developers may structure it however they choose as long as it advances progress toward the Sprint Goal.

question:
Who can prematurely cancel a Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Development Team
The Product Owner
The Stakeholders
The Scrum Master

explanation: Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint, though they may do so under influence from stakeholders, the Developers, or the Scrum Master. A Sprint would be cancelled if its Sprint Goal becomes obsolete, which typically happens when the company changes direction or market/technology conditions shift. Because the Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product and managing the Product Backlog, they are the appropriate person to make this decision.

question:
Which of the following are appropriate topics for discussion during a Sprint Retrospective?
(Choose best 2 answers)

options.text: How the team collaborates
The order of items in the Product Backlog
Identifying high priority process improvements for the next Sprint
Documenting acceptance criteria for items in the next Sprint

explanation: The Sprint Retrospective is the opportunity for the Scrum Team to inspect itself with regard to individuals, interactions, processes, tools, and its Definition of Done, so examining how the team collaborates is directly within its purpose. The event also produces actionable improvements, and the team identifies the most helpful changes to enhance its effectiveness, often adding the highest-priority improvement to the next Sprint Backlog. Ordering the Product Backlog belongs to Product Backlog refinement and the Product Owner's accountability, while defining acceptance criteria is product-focused work rather than process inspection.

question:
User documentation is part of your Definition of Done. However, there are not enough technical writers for all teams. Your Scrum Team does not have a technical writer. What should the Scrum Team do?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Wait until you have a technical writer on your Scrum Team to take care of this.
Let the user documentation remain undone and accumulate until after the last development Sprint. It will then be done by any available technical writers.
The Developers on the Scrum Team should write the user documentation.
Form a separate team of technical writers that will work on an on-demand basis for the various Product Owners. Work order will be first in, first out.

explanation: The Definition of Done is a commitment that every Increment must meet, so any work required to satisfy it—including user documentation—must be completed within the Sprint. Scrum Teams are cross-functional, meaning the Developers collectively possess all the skills necessary to create a usable Increment, and they organize their own work rather than depending on external specialists. Deferring the documentation or waiting for a dedicated writer would produce an Increment that is not truly "Done" and would create undesirable accumulation of undone work.

question:
What are two ways a Scrum Master serves to enable effective Scrum Teams?
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: By keeping high value features high in the Product Backlog.
By facilitating Developer decision-making.
By starting and ending the meetings at the proper time.
By removing impediments that hinder the Scrum Team.

explanation: The Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the Scrum Team, and the Scrum Guide explicitly lists causing the removal of impediments to the Scrum Team's progress as one of the ways they help the team. Facilitating Developer decision-making supports the team's self-management, another core responsibility, since self-managing teams internally decide who does what, when, and how. Ordering the Product Backlog is the Product Owner's accountability, and acting as a clock-keeper to merely start and end meetings on time is not a Scrum Master's role—they coach the team toward effective, valuable events.

question:
Who owns the Sprint Backlog?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Scrum Master.
The Product Owner.
The Developers

explanation: The Sprint Backlog is a plan by and for the Developers, providing a highly visible, real-time picture of the work they plan to accomplish during the Sprint to achieve the Sprint Goal. Because the Developers are the ones who do the work and forecast the functionality to be delivered, they alone own and update the Sprint Backlog throughout the Sprint. This reinforces the self-managing nature of the Scrum Team, where the Developers decide how to turn Product Backlog items into a working Increment.

question:
During the Sprint, the Scrum Master’s role is to do which two of the following:
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: Assign tasks with the Scrum Team
Facilitate inspection and adaptation opportunities as requested or needed
Escalate team conflicts to functional line managers
Monitor the progress of the Developers
Remove impediments
Ensure the Product Owner attends all Scrum events

explanation: The Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the Scrum Team and the organization, and one of their key responsibilities is causing the removal of impediments to the Scrum Team's progress. Additionally, the Scrum Master fosters empiricism by facilitating the Scrum events and other inspection and adaptation opportunities as needed, ensuring they are positive and productive. The Scrum Master does not assign tasks or monitor progress, as the Developers are self-managing and decide how to organize their own work.

question:
The Scrum Master observes the Product Owner is struggling with ordering the Product Backlog. 
What is an appropriate action for the Scrum Master to take?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Present the Product Owner with an ordered Product Backlog to use.
Suggest that the Developers order the Product Backlog to be sure that it is a feasible ordering of work
Offer the Product Owner help in understanding that the goal of ordering the Product Backlog is to maximize value.
Encourage the Product Owner to work with the Developers to see which items technically are fastest to implement.
Suggest the Product Owner extend the Sprint, so he can have more time to order the Product Backlog

explanation: The Scrum Master serves the Product Owner by helping them find techniques for effective Product Backlog management and by coaching them on how to maximize the value of the product. The Product Owner alone is accountable for ordering the Product Backlog, so the Scrum Master should support and educate rather than take over that responsibility or delegate it to the Developers. Ordering by what is fastest or most technically feasible ignores the core principle that the Product Backlog should be ordered to maximize value.

question:
During the Sprint Retrospective a Scrum Team has identified several high priority process improvements. Which of the following statements is most accurate?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The Scrum Master selects the most important process improvement and places it in the Sprint Backlog
The Scrum Team should decline to add a process improvement to the Sprint Backlog when things are running smoothly
The Scrum Team may add the items to the Sprint Backlog for the next Sprint
The Scrum Team should choose at least one high priority process improvement to place in the Product Backlog

explanation: The Scrum Guide states that to ensure continuous improvement, the Scrum Team may include actionable improvements identified during the Sprint Retrospective in the Sprint Backlog for the next Sprint. This is a decision made collaboratively by the whole Scrum Team, not unilaterally by the Scrum Master, and the word "may" reflects that it is encouraged but not strictly mandated. Process improvements belong in the Sprint Backlog where the team can act on them, rather than the Product Backlog, which holds work needed to improve the product.

question:
When might a Sprint be abnormally cancelled? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: When the sales department has an important new opportunity.
When it becomes clear that not everything will be finished by the end of the Sprint.
When the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete.
When the Developers feel that the work is too hard.

explanation: A Sprint can only be cancelled if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete, meaning the work no longer makes sense given changing circumstances. Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint. This is a rare event, since Sprints are short and cancellation is disruptive—incomplete work simply being a normal occurrence does not justify cancellation, as remaining items return to the Product Backlog for reordering.

question:
When can a Developers cancel a Sprint? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: When the Product Owner is absent too often
They can’t. Only Product Owners can cancel Sprints.
When functional expectations are not well understood
When the selected Product Backlog items for the Sprint become unachievable
When a technical dependency cannot be resolved

explanation: According to the Scrum Guide, only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint. This is because the Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product and is the one who can determine when a Sprint Goal has become obsolete. While stakeholders, Developers, or the Scrum Master may influence the decision, the actual cancellation rests solely with the Product Owner.

question:
During Sprint Planning Meeting, the Product Owner and the Developers are unable to reach an understanding about the highest order Product Backlog items. Because of this, the Developers are unable to determine how many Product Backlog items they can forecast for the upcoming Sprint. However, the Product Owner and the Developers are able to agree on a Sprint Goal. Which of the following actions should the Scrum Master support?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Ask everyone to take as much time as needed to analyze the Product Backlog first, and then reconvene another Sprint Planning meeting.
During the next Sprint Retrospective discuss why this happened and what changes will make it less likely to recur.

Continue the Sprint Planning event past its time box until an adequate number of Product Backlog items are well enough understood for the Developers to make a complete forecast. Then start the Sprint.
Cancel the Sprint. Send the entire team to an advanced Scrum training and then start a new Sprint.

Forecast the Product Backlog items that are most likely to meet the Sprint Goal and create the Sprint Backlog. Conclude Sprint Planning and start the development work. Continue to analyze, decompose, and create additional functionality during the Sprint.

explanation: Since the team already agreed on a Sprint Goal, they have the essential foundation to begin the Sprint; the Sprint Backlog is an emergent artifact that can be refined throughout the Sprint as more is learned, so starting work and continuing to decompose items respects empiricism and the Sprint's time box. Sprint Planning is time-boxed and should not be extended, while the Sprint Retrospective is the proper inspect-and-adapt opportunity to examine why the difficulty occurred and identify improvements to prevent recurrence.

question:
Which of the following is required by Scrum?
(Choose all that apply)

options.text: Sprint Retrospective
Members must stand up at the Daily Scrum
Sprint burndown chart
Release planning
All of the above

explanation: The Sprint Retrospective is one of the five required Scrum events, providing a formal opportunity for the Scrum Team to inspect itself and create a plan for improvements to be enacted during the next Sprint. The other options are not mandated by the Scrum Guide: standing up during the Daily Scrum is a common practice but not a rule, the burndown chart is just one optional way to track progress, and release planning is not a prescribed Scrum event. Scrum is intentionally minimal, prescribing only what is necessary while leaving teams free to choose specific techniques.

question: The Product Owner and Scrum Master should not be part of the Developers.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This is false because the Scrum Master or Product Owner can also be part of the Developers if they are actively working on items in the Sprint Backlog. The Scrum Guide describes the Scrum Team as a cohesive unit with no sub-teams or hierarchies, and accountabilities are roles rather than exclusive job titles. As long as the person fulfills their primary accountability, they may also contribute as a Developer.

question:
The Product Backlog is ordered by
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Whatever is deemed most appropriate by the Product Owner.
Least valuable items at the top to most valuable at the bottom.
Size, where small items are at the top and large items are at the bottom.
Items are randomly arranged.
Risk, where safer items are at the top, and riskier items are at the bottom.

explanation: The Product Owner is solely accountable for ordering the Product Backlog and may do so using whatever criteria—value, risk, dependencies, size, or any combination—they judge will best maximize the value delivered by the Scrum Team. The Scrum Guide does not prescribe a fixed ordering rule, leaving the decision to the Product Owner's discretion to achieve goals and missions effectively.

question:
Which two activities will a Product Owner engage in during a Sprint?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Run the Daily Scrum
Update the Sprint burndown chart
Answer questions from the Developers about items in the current Sprint
Prioritize the Developers work on the Sprint Backlog
Work with stakeholders

explanation: During a Sprint the Product Owner remains available to clarify Product Backlog items and answer questions from the Developers, ensuring the work delivers the intended value. The Product Owner is also accountable for stakeholder engagement and managing the Product Backlog, so collaborating with stakeholders is a core ongoing responsibility. The Product Owner does not run the Daily Scrum (that event is for the Developers), nor do they direct how the Developers organize their work, since the Developers self-manage and decide how to turn the Sprint Backlog into an Increment.

question:
When multiple Scrum Teams work together on the same product, 
each team should maintain a separate Product Backlog.

options.text: True
False

explanation: When multiple Scrum Teams work on the same product, they share a single Product Backlog managed by one Product Owner. This ensures a unified vision and consistent prioritization across all teams, allowing them to work toward the same Product Goal. Having separate Product Backlogs would fragment the product and undermine transparency and alignment.

question:
What does it mean for a Scrum Team to be cross-functional?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Developers on the Scrum Team work closely with business analysts, architects, developers, and testers who are not on the team
The Scrum Team includes cross-skilled individuals who are able to contribute to do what is necessary to deliver an Increment.
The Scrum Team is a virtual team drawing from separate teams of business analysts, architects, developers, and testers.
The Scrum Team includes not only developers but also business analysts, architects and testers

explanation: A cross-functional Scrum Team possesses all the skills necessary, within the team itself, to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team. The Scrum Guide states that the Developers collectively have the competencies needed to build a usable Increment, which makes the team self-managing and accountable for the outcome. This is about the team's collective capability to deliver, not about combining separate specialist teams or relying on external roles.

question:
Who determines when it is appropriate to update the Sprint Backlog during a Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Scrum Team
The Project Manager
The Product Owner
The Developers

explanation: The Sprint Backlog is owned exclusively by the Developers, as it is their plan for delivering the Increment and achieving the Sprint Goal. Since they are responsible for managing their own work, only they decide when and how to update the Sprint Backlog as more is learned throughout the Sprint. This reflects the Scrum principle of self-management, where the people doing the work plan and adapt it themselves.

question:
Currently, your Scrum Teams are organized to address a single layer only (for example, front end, middle tier, back end, and interfaces).
What are three things to consider when deciding to move away from such component teams toward feature teams?
(Choose the best three answers)

options.text: You cannot do Scrum without feature teams.
Productivity may suffer when making this kind of move.
Getting support from the business side first helps.
Feature teams have less communication overhead.
With feature teams, it is easier to calculate the productivity per team.

explanation: Transitioning from component teams to feature teams is an organizational change that typically causes a temporary dip in productivity as teams learn to work across the full stack and develop new skills. Securing support from the business side is valuable because this kind of structural change requires organizational backing to succeed. Feature teams reduce communication overhead since a single team can deliver an entire feature end-to-end without coordinating handoffs across multiple component teams, supporting the Scrum principle that a Scrum Team should be cross-functional and able to create a valuable, usable Increment each Sprint.

question:
Which of the below are accountabilities on a Scrum Team? 
(choose the best three answers)

options.text: Product Owner.
Scrum Master.
Customers.
Users.
Developers.

explanation: The Scrum Guide defines a Scrum Team as consisting of exactly three accountabilities: one Product Owner, one Scrum Master, and the Developers. There are no sub-teams or hierarchies, and these are the only roles within the team itself. Customers and users are important stakeholders who interact with the Scrum Team but are not members of it, so they hold no accountability within the team structure.

question:
At the seventh Sprint Review, the stakeholders are disappointed and angry. They have determined that the product or system being built will not meet their needs and will cost more than they are willing to spend. What factors likely led to this?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: The project management office (PMO) has not been engaged adequately.
The Product Owner has not been keeping the stakeholders aware of the progress of the project
The stakeholders were not allowed to prioritize Product Backlog items.
The stakeholders haven’t been using the Sprint Reviews to inspect and evaluate progress

explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for managing stakeholder expectations and ensuring transparency around the product's progress and value, so failing to keep stakeholders informed would lead to misaligned expectations. The Sprint Review is the formal event where stakeholders collaborate with the Scrum Team to inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog; if stakeholders weren't actively inspecting and providing feedback across the previous six reviews, problems with direction and cost would go undetected until too late. Together, these failures break the empirical pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

question:
When is implementation of Product Backlog item considered complete? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: When QA reports the item passes all acceptance criteria
When the item has no work remaining in order to be potentially released
At the end of the Sprint
When all work in the Sprint Backlog related to the item is finished

explanation: A Product Backlog item is complete when it meets the Definition of Done, which ensures transparency about the work being potentially releasable. Passing acceptance criteria or finishing related Sprint Backlog tasks does not guarantee the item meets the quality standards required by the Definition of Done. The end of the Sprint is a time boundary, not a measure of an item's completeness—only Increments that satisfy the Definition of Done are considered truly finished.

question:
The CEO asks the Developers to add a “very important” item to a Sprint that is in progress. 
What should the Developers do?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Add the item to the current Sprint and drop an item of equal size
Inform the Product Owner so they can work with the CEO
Add the item to the next Sprint
Add the item to the current Sprint without any adjustments

explanation: The Product Owner is solely accountable for managing the Product Backlog and is the one person responsible for ordering and deciding what work the team takes on. Stakeholders, including a CEO, cannot directly inject work into a Sprint; doing so would undermine the Developers' focus and the integrity of the Sprint Goal. By redirecting the request to the Product Owner, the Developers respect the value of focus and ensure the right person evaluates the item's priority against existing work.

question:
How should a Scrum Team deal with non-functional requirements?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Ensure every Increment meets them.
Assign them to the lead developers on the team.
Make sure the release department understands these requirements, but it is not the Scrum Team's responsibility.
Manage them during the Integration Sprint prior to the Release Sprint.

explanation: Non-functional requirements (such as performance, security, and reliability) are part of the Definition of Done, which defines the quality measures required for the Increment. Because every Increment must be usable and meet the Definition of Done, the Scrum Team must ensure that each Increment satisfies these requirements rather than deferring them to a later phase or delegating them to specific individuals. Scrum has no "Integration" or "Release" Sprints—quality is built into the product continuously.

question:
As the Scrum Team matures, what are possible outcomes?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Sprint Retrospective is likely to last longer than 4 hours.
The Scrum Team is able to work with a more demanding Definition of Done
The Scrum Master is no longer needed.
The Daily Scrum is no longer needed.

explanation: As a Scrum Team matures, it gains the skills, discipline, and quality practices to expand its Definition of Done, building greater rigor and transparency into the Increment. The Scrum Master remains valuable to a mature team by serving the team and organization in deeper ways, and the Daily Scrum continues as an essential inspection-and-adaptation event. The Sprint Retrospective is timeboxed to a maximum of three hours for a one-month Sprint, so it would never extend to four hours.

question:
What are typical Product Owner activities during Sprint 0?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Creating a project plan based on the defined timeline.
Ensuring there is enough work to do for at least three Sprints.
Creating User Stories based on the requirements document.
Allocating enough resources before starting Sprint 1.
There is no such thing as Sprint 0.

explanation: There is no concept of a "Sprint 0" in Scrum; the framework defines only Sprints of consistent length, each of which must produce a valuable, usable Increment. The idea of a preparatory Sprint dedicated to upfront planning, requirements gathering, or resource allocation contradicts Scrum's empirical, iterative approach where work and understanding emerge incrementally. From the very first Sprint, the Scrum Team is expected to deliver a "Done" Increment rather than spend time on activities like project plans or resource allocation that reflect traditional, predictive methods.

question: The Product Owner makes sure the Developers select enough from the Product Backlog for a Sprint to satisfy the stakeholders.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This is false because the Developers alone are responsible for determining how much work they can take on during the Sprint—forecasting the Product Backlog items they believe they can complete. The Product Owner influences the work by ordering the Product Backlog, but cannot dictate or pressure the Developers into selecting more than they judge feasible. This protects the self-managing nature of the Scrum Team and supports realistic, sustainable commitments rather than satisfying stakeholders at the expense of the team's autonomy.

question:
At the end of a Sprint, a Product Backlog Item worked on during the Sprint does not meet the Definition of Done. What two things should happen with the undone Product Backlog item? 
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Do not include the item in the Increment this Sprint.
Put it on the Product Backlog for the Product Owner to decide what to do with it.
Review the item, add the done part of the estimate to the velocity and create a Story for the remaining work.
If the stakeholders agree, the Product Owner can accept it and release it to the users.

explanation: An item that does not meet the Definition of Done is not part of the Increment and cannot be released, because only work that satisfies the Definition of Done is potentially releasable and ensures transparency over what is truly complete. The unfinished item is returned to the Product Backlog, where the Product Owner—who is accountable for ordering and managing the Product Backlog—decides how and when the remaining work will be addressed.

question:
When should the Developers on a Scrum Team be replaced? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: As needed, while taking into account a short-term reduction in productivity.
Every Sprint to promote shared learning.
Never, it reduces productivity.
As needed, with no special allowance for changes in productivity.

explanation: Scrum Teams should remain stable to build cohesion and shared understanding, but changes to membership are sometimes necessary and are permitted as needed. However, changing team composition disrupts the team's established working relationships and shared context, which typically lowers productivity in the short term as the team re-forms and adapts. Recognizing this trade-off, the decision to replace Developers should account for the temporary dip in productivity rather than assuming no impact or refusing change altogether.

question: Scrum has a role called "project manager

options.text: True
False

explanation: Scrum defines only three accountabilities: the Product Owner, the Scrum Master, and the Developers. There is no "project manager" role in Scrum, as the responsibilities traditionally held by a project manager are distributed across these three accountabilities. This reflects Scrum's emphasis on self-management, where the Scrum Team collectively decides how to accomplish its work rather than relying on a single managing authority.

question:
Which statement best describes a Product Owner´s responsibility?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Keep stakeholders from distracting the Developers
Directing the Developers
Ensuring that the work meets the commitments to the stakeholders
Optimizing the value of the work the Scrum Team does

explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product resulting from the work of the Scrum Team. This is achieved primarily through effective Product Backlog management—ordering items and clearly communicating the Product Goal. The Product Owner does not direct the Developers, who are self-managing and decide how to turn Product Backlog items into Increments themselves.

question:
Which answer best describes the topics covered in Sprint Planning?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Who is the team and what team member roles will be.
What to do and who will do it.
What can be done, how to do it, and why to do it
What went wrong in the last Sprint and what to do differently this Sprint.
How conditions have changed and how the Product Backlog should evolve

explanation: Sprint Planning addresses three topics: Why is this Sprint valuable (the Sprint Goal that gives purpose), What can be done this Sprint (selecting Product Backlog items), and How will the chosen work get done (the Developers' plan for delivering an Increment). Reviewing what went wrong in the previous Sprint belongs to the Sprint Retrospective, while inspecting how conditions have changed and adapting the backlog is part of ongoing Product Backlog refinement and the Sprint Review.

question:
The Sprint Review is mainly an inspect and adapt opportunity for which group?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: The Product Owner and management.
The Developers and management.
The Scrum Team and stakeholders.
The Developers and stakeholders.
The Product Owner and stakeholders
The Product Owner and Developers

explanation: The Sprint Review is a collaborative working session where the entire Scrum Team and key stakeholders inspect the outcome of the Sprint and the current state of the Product Backlog, then adapt what to do next based on feedback. It is not a one-sided presentation but a chance for the Scrum Team and stakeholders to collaborate on future direction to optimize value. Excluding any of these participants would undermine the transparency and adaptation the event is designed to foster.

question:
What two factors are best considered when establishing the Sprint length? 
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: The level of uncertainty over the technology to be used
The frequency at which team formation can be changed
The risk of being disconnected from the stakeholders
The organization has mandated similar length Sprints

explanation: Sprint length should be chosen to limit risk and maintain alignment with stakeholders. Higher technological uncertainty argues for shorter Sprints, since they limit cost and risk to a smaller time-box and provide more frequent opportunities to inspect and adapt. Likewise, shorter Sprints reduce the risk of drifting away from stakeholder needs by ensuring more frequent feedback through the Sprint Review, supporting empirical process control. Organizational mandates and team-formation frequency are not appropriate drivers for determining Sprint length.

question:
Which Scrum events are timeboxed? 
(choose the best three answers)

options.text: Refinement
Sprint Review.
Sprint Retrospective.
Sprint Testing.
Sprint Planning.
Release Retrospective.
Release Testing.

explanation: All Scrum events are timeboxed to create regularity and minimize the need for other meetings. Sprint Planning is timeboxed to a maximum of eight hours for a one-month Sprint, the Sprint Review to a maximum of four hours, and the Sprint Retrospective to a maximum of three hours, with shorter durations for shorter Sprints. Refinement is an ongoing activity rather than a formal Scrum event, and "Sprint Testing" and "Release Retrospective" are not Scrum events at all.

question:
What is the purpose of a Sprint Review?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: To inspect the product Increment with the stakeholders and collect feedback on next steps.
To review the Scrum Team’s activities and processes during the Sprint.
To build team spirit.
To take time to judge the validity of the project.

explanation: The Sprint Review is held at the end of the Sprint to inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog if needed. It is a working session where the Scrum Team and stakeholders collaborate on what was accomplished and what could be done next to optimize value. Reviewing the team's processes belongs to the Sprint Retrospective, not the Sprint Review.

question:
The timebox for the Sprint Review is
(choose the best answer)

options.text: 2 hours.
1 day.
4 hours for a monthly Sprint. For shorter Sprints it is usually shorter.
As long as needed.
4 hours and longer as needed.

explanation: The Sprint Review is timeboxed to a maximum of four hours for a one-month Sprint, and for shorter Sprints the event is usually shorter. Timeboxing is a core Scrum principle that ensures events are focused, efficient, and do not exceed their allotted duration, supporting transparency and empiricism without wasting time.

question: It is mandatory that the product Increment be released to production at the end of each Sprint.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This is false because Scrum requires that each Increment be a usable, "Done" product that *could* be released, but the actual decision and timing of release is a business and product strategy choice made by the Product Owner. A Sprint may produce multiple Increments, and releasing to production can happen during the Sprint or be deferred, as long as the Increment meets the Definition of Done and is potentially releasable.

question:
What does it mean for a Scrum Team to be cross-functional? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Developers work closely with business analysts, architects, developers and testers who are not on the team.
The Developers include also business analysts, architects and testers.
The Scrum Team include cross-skilled individuals who are able to contribute to do what is necessary to deliver an Increment of software
The Scrum Team is a virtual team drawing from separate teams of business analysts, architects, developers and testers.

explanation: A cross-functional Scrum Team possesses all the competencies needed to create value each Sprint without depending on others outside the team. This means the team collectively has the skills required to do the work and deliver a "Done" Increment, rather than relying on separate roles or external specialists. The emphasis is on the team's combined capability to accomplish whatever is necessary, supporting self-management and reducing dependencies.

question:
Which of the following are true about the Product Owner?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: The Product Owner can be represented by a committee or a team of people.
The Scrum Team can have multiple Product Owners.
The Product Owner is accountable for ordering the Product Backlog.
The Product Owner is one person.

explanation: The Scrum Guide explicitly states that the Product Owner is one person, not a committee or a team, ensuring clear and single accountability for maximizing the value of the product. Although the Product Owner may represent the needs of many stakeholders in the Product Backlog, decision-making authority rests with that single individual. The Product Owner is solely accountable for managing the Product Backlog, which includes ordering its items to best achieve goals and missions.

question:
Which of the following are time-boxed events in Scrum?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Sprint Planning
Daily Scrum
Sprint Review
Sprint Retrospective
All of the above

explanation: All Scrum events are time-boxed to create regularity and minimize the need for meetings not defined in Scrum. Sprint Planning is time-boxed to a maximum of eight hours for a one-month Sprint, the Daily Scrum to 15 minutes, the Sprint Review to a maximum of four hours, and the Sprint Retrospective to a maximum of three hours, with all durations typically shorter for shorter Sprints. The Sprint itself is also a time-boxed container event holding all the others.

question:
The Product Backlog is ordered by
 (choose the best answer)

options.text: Items are randomly arranged.
Whatever is deemed most appropriate by the Product Owner.
Risk, where safer Items are at the top, and riskier Items are at the bottom.
Least valuable Items at the top to most valuable at the bottom.
Size, where small Items are at the top and large Items are at the bottom.

explanation: The Product Owner is solely accountable for managing the Product Backlog, which includes ordering its items. While factors like value, risk, dependencies, and size may inform the ordering, the Scrum Guide does not prescribe a fixed ordering scheme—it leaves the decision to the Product Owner to order items in whatever way best maximizes the value of the product and the work of the Developers.

question:
What are Developers responsible for?
(Choose the best 2 answers)

options.text: Selecting the Product Owner
Resolving internal conflicts
Reporting productivity
Organizing the work required to meet the Sprint Goal

explanation: The Developers are a self-managing team, meaning they internally decide who does what, when, and how, which includes organizing the work needed to meet the Sprint Goal and resolving conflicts among themselves without relying on external authority. Selecting the Product Owner is not the Developers' responsibility, and Scrum does not require reporting productivity—the focus is on delivering a valuable, usable Increment each Sprint.

question:
You are the Scrum Master on a newly formed Scrum Team. Which two of the following activities would probably help the team in starting up? 
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Ensure the team understands they need a Definition of Done
Ensure the team members have compatible personalities
Have the development managers for each Developer introduce their direct reports and go over their responsibilities on the Scrum Team
Introduce a bonus system for the top performers in the team
Ask the Product Owner to discuss the product, its vision, history, goals, and context, as well as answer questions

explanation: A new Scrum Team needs a shared Definition of Done to create transparency about quality and ensure a usable, valuable Increment is produced each Sprint; without it, the team cannot understand when work is truly complete. Equally important, the Product Owner sharing the product vision, history, goals, and context aligns the team toward the Product Goal and maximizes the value the team will deliver. The other options conflict with Scrum—the team is self-managing with no managers assigning responsibilities, individual bonus systems undermine the collaborative, shared-accountability nature of the team, and "compatible personalities" is not a Scrum concern.

question:
What is the maximum length of a Sprint?
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Not so long that the risk is unacceptable to the Product Owner.
Not so long that other business events can't be readily synchronized with the development work.
No more than one calendar month.
All_of these answers are correct.

explanation: The Scrum Guide explicitly states that Sprints are fixed-length events of one month or less to create consistency, and that no single Sprint should exceed one calendar month. Limiting the Sprint to a calendar month or less helps keep complexity and risk manageable, ensures the Product Goal remains valid, and provides frequent inspection and adaptation opportunities. While managing risk and synchronizing with business events are benefits of shorter Sprints, the defined maximum boundary is one calendar month.

question:
What would be good ways of creating Development Teams that would support the Scrum Values?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Project Managers will allocate individuals to specific teams.
Bring all the developers or existing teams together and let them propose how to organize into the new structure and self-organize into Development Teams.
The operations manager determines the new team structures and assignments.
Direct managers personally re-assign current reports to new teams.

explanation: Self-organization is a foundational principle of Scrum—teams choose how best to accomplish their work rather than being directed by others outside the team. Allowing developers to propose and form their own teams reinforces the Scrum Values of commitment, courage, and respect, since people are more invested in arrangements they help create. Having managers or other external roles dictate team structures undermines this self-organizing nature and the autonomy that makes Scrum Teams effective.

question:
The Sprint length should be
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Short enough to keep the business risk acceptable to the Product Owner.
Short enough to be able to synchronize the development work with other business events.
No more than one month.
All of these answers are correct.

explanation: The Scrum Guide states that Sprints are fixed-length events of one month or less, providing the upper boundary that keeps the cost of risk to no more than one calendar month. Shorter Sprints can be employed to generate more learning cycles and limit the risk of cost and effort to a smaller time frame, which keeps business risk acceptable to the Product Owner. Aligning Sprint length with other business cadences also supports the empirical inspect-and-adapt cycle, so all of these considerations legitimately influence the chosen Sprint length.

question:
Which outcome is expected as Scrum Teams mature?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: A Scrum Master is no longer needed since they are a mature team now
They will improve their Definition of Done to include more stringent criteria
There is no need for a time-boxed Sprint, since time-boxes are only for new Scrum Teams
The Sprint Retrospectives will grow to be longer than 4 hours
Sprint Reviewers will no longer be needed

explanation: As Scrum Teams mature, their Definition of Done is expected to expand to encompass more comprehensive and stringent criteria, reflecting an increased ability to deliver higher quality, valuable Increments. This embodies the principle of continuous improvement central to empiricism and the Scrum value of commitment. The other options contradict Scrum rules—the Scrum Master, time-boxed Sprints, and stakeholder feedback at the Sprint Review remain essential regardless of team maturity.

question:
Which of the following are true about the length of a Sprint?
(Choose the best answers)

options.text: The length of the Sprint should be proportional to the work that is done in between Sprints
Sprint length is determined during Sprint Planning and should be long enough to make sure the Developers can deliver what is to be accomplished in the upcoming Sprint
Sprint length is determined during Sprint Planning and should hold the time it will take to code the planned features in the upcoming Sprint but does not include time for any testing
All Sprints must be one month or less

explanation: Sprints are fixed-length events of one month or less that create consistency, and a new Sprint starts immediately after the conclusion of the previous one. Sprint length is not determined during Sprint Planning, nor is it varied based on the work to be done—it remains a constant timebox. Furthermore, every Sprint must produce a usable Increment that meets the Definition of Done, which includes all work such as testing, not just coding.

question:
Which do self-managing Developers choose?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: When to release, based on its progress
How to best accomplish the work
Sprint length
Product Backlog ordering
Stakeholders for the Sprint Review

explanation: Self-management in Scrum means the Developers internally decide who does the work, when, and how. The Scrum Guide specifically states that the Scrum Team is cross-functional and self-managing, choosing how best to accomplish their work rather than being directed by others. Decisions like Sprint length are part of the Scrum framework, while Product Backlog ordering and release timing fall to the Product Owner, who is accountable for maximizing value.

question:
The length of a Sprint should be 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Short enough to keep the business risk acceptable to the Product Owner.
Short enough to be able to synchronize the development work with other business events.
One month or less.
All of the above

explanation: Sprints are limited to one calendar month or less, which is a fixed rule in the Scrum Guide to ensure inspection and adaptation happen at predictable intervals. Within that maximum, the appropriate Sprint length also depends on keeping the business risk—the amount of work invested before the next inspection point—acceptable to the Product Owner, and on aligning the cadence with relevant business events. Because all of these considerations legitimately influence Sprint length, the best answer encompasses each of them.

question:
How much work must the Developers complete for each Product Backlog item they select for a Sprint? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Analysis, design, programming, testing and documentation.
As much as it can fit into the Sprint.
All development work and at least some testing.
Enough so that each Product Backlog item they select meets the Definition of Done.

explanation: The Definition of Done is the formal, shared standard that describes when work is complete and creates transparency about the state of the Increment. Specific activities like analysis, design, or testing may vary between teams and items, so what truly matters is that each item satisfies the agreed Definition of Done. This ensures every Increment delivered is potentially releasable and meets the team's quality standards.

question:
Five new Scrum Teams have been created to build one product. A few of the Developers on one of the Scrum Teams ask the Scrum Master how to coordinate their work with the other teams. What should the Scrum Master do?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Visit the five teams each day to inspect that their Sprint Backlogs are aligned
Teach them that it is their responsibility to work with the other teams to create an integrated Increment that is inclusive of all five team’s work.
Teach the Product Owner to work with the lead developers on ordering Product Backlog in a way to avoid too much overlap during a Sprint.
Collect the Sprint tasks from the teams at the end of their Sprint Planning and merge that into a consolidated plan for the entire Sprint.

explanation: When multiple Scrum Teams work on the same product, they share one Product Backlog, one Product Goal, and one common Definition of Done, and must produce a single integrated Increment. The Developers themselves are responsible for self-organizing and coordinating with the other teams to achieve this integration, rather than relying on the Scrum Master to centrally manage or merge their work. The Scrum Master serves by teaching the teams self-management and helping them understand this shared accountability.

question:
The timebox for a Daily Scrum is? 
(choose the best answer)

options.text: Two minutes per person.
15 minutes.
4 hours.
The same time of day every day.
15 minutes for a 4-week Sprint. For shorter Sprints it is usually shorter.

explanation: The Daily Scrum is timeboxed to 15 minutes regardless of Sprint length, providing a consistent and focused event for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt their plan for the next day of work. Unlike other events whose timeboxes scale with Sprint duration, the Daily Scrum is always held for a maximum of 15 minutes. While the Scrum Guide recommends holding it at the same time and place each day to reduce complexity, that consistency is a practice rather than the defined timebox itself.

question:
Which of the following activities will a Product Owner engage in during a Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Run the Daily Scrum
Update management on what is being worked on.
Prioritze the Developers work on the Sprint Backlog
Answer questions from the Developers about items in the current Sprint

explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product, which includes clearly communicating Product Backlog items and being available to clarify their meaning. During the Sprint, the Developers may need clarification about the items they are building, so the Product Owner answers their questions to ensure the work delivers the intended value. The Product Owner does not run the Daily Scrum (which is for the Developers) nor direct how the Developers organize the Sprint Backlog, since the Developers are self-managing and solely responsible for managing their own work.

question:
You are the Scrum Master of four Scrum Teams working from the same Product Backlog. Several of the developers come to you complaining that work identified for the upcoming two Sprints will require full-time commitment from a technical specialist who is external to the teams.
What should the Scrum Master consider in this situation?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: The need to have enough work to keep all Developers busy.
The ability of the Scrum Teams to produce integrated Increments.
The desire to maintain a stable velocity.
The benefit of Developers figuring out a solution for themselves

explanation: The primary goal each Sprint is delivering a valuable, usable, integrated Increment, so the Scrum Master must consider whether dependency on an external specialist threatens the teams' ability to produce that integrated work—especially when multiple teams share one Product Backlog and must integrate into a single product. At the same time, Scrum relies on self-managing teams that organize their own work and solve their own problems, so the Scrum Master should encourage the Developers to figure out a solution themselves rather than immediately resolving the impediment for them. Concerns like keeping everyone busy or maintaining a stable velocity contradict Scrum's focus on value delivery rather than utilization or output metrics.

question:
Who creates a Product Backlog Item’s estimate?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The Scrum Master
The Product Owner with input from the Developers.
The Developers, alone
The Developers after clarifying requirements with the Product Owner
The most senior people in the organization, including architects and subject matter experts

explanation: The Developers are responsible for sizing and estimating the work, as they are the ones who will perform it—no one else can impose estimates on them. However, to estimate accurately they need a clear understanding of the Product Backlog items, so they collaborate with the Product Owner to clarify requirements and trade-offs. This reflects the Scrum value of collaboration while preserving the Developers' accountability for the work.

question:
The Developers can cancel the Sprint
(choose the best answer)

options.text: When the availability of the Product Owner is less than expected.
When the requirements for the work to be done become unclear or not understood.
It can’t. Only Product Owners can cancel Sprints.
When the Sprint Backlog is determined to be unachievable.
When too many impediments occur.

explanation: Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint, as stated in the Scrum Guide, since they are accountable for maximizing the value of the product and the Sprint Goal. A Sprint would only be cancelled if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete, but that decision rests solely with the Product Owner. The Developers, who are responsible for managing the Sprint Backlog and delivering the Increment, do not hold this authority.

question:
For the purpose of transparency, 
when does Scrum say a valuable and useful Increment must be available?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: After the Acceptance Testing phase.
Every 3 Sprints.
At the end of every Sprint.
Before the Release Sprint.
When the Product Owner asks to create one.

explanation: A usable Increment must be produced by the end of every Sprint, ensuring that empirical inspection and adaptation can occur regularly based on real, working product. Scrum does not recognize concepts like "Acceptance Testing phases" or "Release Sprints," and waiting multiple Sprints would undermine the transparency needed for inspection. Each Sprint delivers at least one Increment that meets the Definition of Done, making it potentially releasable regardless of whether the Product Owner chooses to release it.

question: Multiple Scrum Teams working in the same project must have the same Sprint Start?

options.text: True
False

explanation: Nothing in the Scrum framework requires multiple teams working on the same product to share the same Sprint cadence or start date. While teams often choose to synchronize Sprints for easier integration and a unified Increment, this is an organizational decision rather than a rule. Scrum prescribes the elements of the framework but leaves the specifics of how multiple teams coordinate up to those teams.

question:
What would be two boundaries, defined in Scrum, that give guidance for teams to effectively self organize?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Timeboxing the events in Scrum to allow for regular inspection and adaptation creating opportunities to adjust course in any given path.
Clearly defined functional teams within the Development Team to define handoff phases during development.
Creating an integrated and potentially shippable Increment by the end of each Sprint.
Having a mixture of different levels of skills and experience to promote domain knowledge sharing.

explanation: Timeboxes provide a structural boundary within which the team is free to organize its own work, while still ensuring regular inspection and adaptation to stay on course. Likewise, the requirement to produce a "Done," potentially releasable Increment each Sprint sets a clear goal boundary that focuses self-organization toward delivering value without dictating how the work gets done. Functional sub-teams and handoffs would contradict the principle that the Development Team is cross-functional and accountable as a whole, and a mix of skill levels, while beneficial, is not a defined Scrum boundary for self-organization.

question:
One of the Scrum events is the Daily Scrum. What are two intended outcomes of the Daily Scrum?
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: Identification of impediments that may prevent the Developers from achieving the Sprint Goal.
A status report for the upper management indicating what each individual has done, will be doing, and what is impeding him/her.
A shared understanding of the most important work to be undertaken next to achieve the best possible progress toward the Sprint Goal.
An updated Scrum board to make Sprint progress transparent for the stakeholders.
An updated Scrum board to make Sprint progress transparent for the stakeholders.

explanation: The Daily Scrum is an event for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog as necessary, adjusting the upcoming planned work. This naturally surfaces impediments blocking the path to the Sprint Goal and produces a shared understanding of the most important work to tackle next. It is not a status meeting for management or a report to stakeholders; its purpose is to improve communication and enable the Developers to self-manage their plan for the day.

question:
Who can cancel a Sprint?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: The stakeholders
The Scrum Master
The Developers
The Product Owner

explanation: A Sprint can be cancelled if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete, and only the Product Owner has the authority to do this. Because the Product Owner is accountable for maximizing the value of the product and managing the Product Backlog, they are best positioned to judge when a Sprint Goal no longer makes sense to pursue. While others may influence this decision, the cancellation authority rests solely with the Product Owner.

question:
Your organization has formed a new Scrum Team and has assigned you as the Scrum Master.
In what ways would you help the team start? 
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Ensure the Scrum Team members have compatible personalities, have the tech leads clarify the expectations and responsibilities of each role, and propose a performance rewards system.

Have the Scrum Team members introduce their background experience with each other, ask the Product Owner to discuss the product and answer questions, and ensure the team understands the need for a Definition of “Done.”

explanation: A Scrum Master serves the team by fostering self-management, collaboration, and a shared understanding of the product and its value. Helping members get to know one another, enabling the Product Owner to convey the product vision, and establishing a Definition of Done all support transparency and the team's ability to deliver a valuable, usable Increment. The alternative focuses on individual roles, fixed responsibilities, and rewards, which contradict the cross-functional, self-managing nature of a Scrum Team and the Scrum value of collaboration.

question:
When does a new Sprint begin?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: Immediately after the next Sprint Planning.
Immediately after the end of the previous Sprint.
It depends on the Product Owner.
Every Monday.

explanation: The Scrum Guide states that a new Sprint starts immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint, with no gaps in between. This continuous cadence ensures a consistent rhythm of work and inspection, keeping the team in a sustainable, predictable flow. Sprints are not triggered by a calendar day, the Product Owner's discretion, or the completion of Sprint Planning—they simply follow one another back-to-back.

question: Scrum Master is a "management" position?

options.text: True
False

explanation: The Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the Scrum Team and the larger organization, and the role is accountable for the team's effectiveness. The Scrum Master manages by serving others, removing impediments, coaching, and fostering an environment where Scrum is understood and enacted—this is a form of leadership and management focused on enabling the team rather than commanding it. In this sense, the Scrum Master holds a management position grounded in servant-leadership.

question:
Developers are required to deliver a done Increment by the end of a Sprint. 
Select two statements that explain what “Done” means. 
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: Whatever the Product Owner defines as quality.
No work left from the definition of “Done”.
Ready for integration.
All work the Team is willing to do.
All work to create software that is ready to be released to end users.

explanation: "Done" is defined by the Increment meeting the Definition of Done, meaning no remaining work exists relative to that shared, agreed-upon standard of quality. A Done Increment must be usable and in a releasable state, ensuring transparency and that it could actually be delivered to end users. The Definition of Done is a formal commitment to the Increment, not an arbitrary choice by the Product Owner or based merely on what the team feels like doing.

question:
Which three of the following are true about Scrum? 
(Choose the best 3 answers)

options.text: Each component of Scrum serves a specific purpose and is essential to Scrum’s success and your usage of Scrum to develop complex products
Scrum is a framework for developing and sustaining complex products
Scrum is a methodology where you can pick and choose which parts of Scrum you think will work for your environment
Scrum is based on empirical process control theory.
Scrum is like traditional processes but with self-organization to replace Project Managers.

explanation: Scrum is intentionally defined as a lightweight framework—not a complete methodology—for developing, delivering, and sustaining complex products, which is why simply picking and choosing parts misrepresents it. Each element (roles, events, artifacts, and rules) binds together and serves a specific purpose; omitting any piece undermines Scrum and conceals problems rather than revealing them. Scrum is founded on empiricism, where knowledge comes from experience and decisions are based on what is observed, supported by the three pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation. This empirical, framework-based nature distinguishes Scrum from traditional, predictive processes.

question:
Which two of these situations best describe that a Developers is self-organizing?
(choose the best two answers)

options.text: The Development Team creates its own Sprint Backlog, reflecting all work that is part of the Definition of “Done”.
Management is invited to the Daily Scrum for a progress update and subsequently works with the Scrum Master to optimize the plan for the next day.
The Development Team members are strictly working within the boundaries of their function description, and are handing off work in a timely fashion to the other roles within the team.
The Development Team invites the right external people to the Sprint Planning to help them create a complete and detailed Sprint Backlog before the meeting time-box expires.
Development Team members collaboratively select and re-plan their work during the Sprint.

explanation: Self-organizing teams choose how best to accomplish their work, rather than being directed by others outside the team. The Developers own the Sprint Backlog and create it themselves to deliver an Increment that meets the Definition of "Done," demonstrating their authority over the "how" of the work. Likewise, collaboratively selecting and continuously re-planning work throughout the Sprint reflects the daily inspection and adaptation of their plan to meet the Sprint Goal, which is a hallmark of self-management.

question:
You have just been hired by a company new to Scrum. Your management has assigned you to be the Scrum Master of six new Scrum Teams. These teams will build one product. Select two conditions you should strive for in this scenario. 
(Choose the best two answers)

options.text: There should be six Product Owners reporting to chief Product Owner
Each Scrum Team should have a separate Product Backlog
The product has one Product Backlog
There should be six Product Owners, one for each Scrum Team
There should be only one Product Owner

explanation: A product has exactly one Product Backlog and one Product Owner, regardless of how many Scrum Teams work on it. The single Product Backlog provides one authoritative source of work that maximizes the value of the product and ensures transparency for everyone, while a single Product Owner is accountable for maximizing that value and managing the ordering of the backlog. Splitting the backlog or having multiple Product Owners would fragment accountability and undermine a coherent product vision.

question:
When is it most appropriate for a Scrum Team to change the Definition of Done?
(Choose the best answer)

options.text: During Sprint Planning
Prior to starting a new project.
Prior to starting a new Sprint.
During the Sprint Retrospective

explanation: The Sprint Retrospective is the formal opportunity for the Scrum Team to inspect how it works and plan improvements to increase quality and effectiveness, which includes adapting the Definition of Done. Since the Definition of Done is a commitment to quality created by the Scrum Team, refining it during the Retrospective ensures the whole team agrees and applies the updated standard in future Sprints. This aligns with Scrum's empirical principle of inspection and adaptation to continuously improve the product and process.
